note: Taxonomy of Arabic morphology and syntax skills. Internal labels are for LLM reasoning only and must never appear in user-facing UI. Examples are ordered: index 0 is the lead-in for teaching. For Quranic verses, grammar framings must not ascribe agency to subjects in ways that contradict the verse's theological meaning — describe what the verb shape expresses, leaving the actual cause to the verse's broader meaning where appropriate.

skills.user_facing_pattern: The bare verb. Three root letters with vowel marks and nothing added — no doubling, no prefix, no infix. Most common shapes are CaCaCa (فَعَلَ) like كَتَبَ، خَلَقَ، نَصَرَ; CaCiCa (فَعِلَ) like سَمِعَ، عَلِمَ; or CaCuCa (فَعُلَ) like كَبُرَ، كَرُمَ.
skills.semantic_note: This is the unmodified meaning of the root. No causative, no reflexive, no seeking — just the action plainly. The vowel pattern often correlates loosely with action types: a-a verbs tend toward active doing (كَتَبَ wrote, خَلَقَ created), a-i verbs lean toward states or sensations (سَمِعَ heard, عَلِمَ knew), and a-u verbs tend toward stative qualities (كَبُرَ was great, كَرُمَ was honorable).
skills.why_explanation: Every Arabic root can take multiple verb shapes — up to ten standard ones. Form I is the foundation. It carries the root's core meaning before any morphological reshaping. Once you know the Form I meaning of a root, the other forms become systematic shifts of it: II and IV add 'cause,' V and VI and VII and VIII add reflexive or middle-voice flavors, X adds 'seek' or 'consider.' Recognizing Form I is mostly about noticing a verb that's just three root letters plus vowel marks — no extras layered on.

examples.translation: He created man from a clinging clot.
examples.note: Three letters, simple a-a vowel pattern: خَلَقَ. No prefix, no doubling, no infix — the bare verb. The doer is Allah (implicit from context). Pure Form I.

examples.translation: Indeed, We created you from a male and a female.
examples.note: Same root and same Form I as the lead, but with different endings: ـنا is 'we,' ـكم is 'you all.' The base verb shape خَلَقَ is still visible — the endings just attach who did it and to whom.

examples.translation: And when your Lord said to the angels.
examples.note: قَالَ is one of the most repeated verbs in the Quran. Form I, but the middle root letter is و — a 'weak' letter that's been folded into a long ا in the verb. The base shape is still Form I.

examples.translation: The child drank the water.
examples.note: Sound root, a-i pattern (شَرِبَ). Form I across all of Arabic — Quranic and modern. The same word the Quran uses for drinking.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs where the middle root letter is doubled (carries a shadda).
skills.semantic_note: Often makes the action causative ('cause to do'), or intensifies it, or makes it more deliberate or repeated. عَلِمَ = 'he knew' (Form I) → عَلَّمَ = 'he taught' (Form II — caused to know).
skills.why_explanation: Form II is one of the two main causative forms in Arabic (the other is Form IV). The shape is built by doubling the middle root letter — a shadda sits over it. The doubling does the semantic work: it bunches the action up, intensifies it, or turns it from 'X happened' into 'caused X to happen.' For some roots Form II is the only available causative; for others both II and IV exist with subtly different shades — Form II tends to feel more thorough or repeated, Form IV more like a single completed act. Which one a given root prefers is something you absorb from exposure rather than from a rule.

examples.translation: He taught man what he did not know.
examples.note: Form I عَلِمَ = 'he knew.' Form II عَلَّمَ doubles the middle letter and the meaning shifts: 'caused [someone] to know' = taught. The verse contrasts both forms in one breath: عَلَّمَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ — taught man what he didn't know.

examples.translation: And We sent it down gradually, in stages.
examples.note: Form I نَزَلَ = 'came down.' Form II نَزَّلَ = 'sent down' — causative. But Form II also adds the gradual/repeated flavor here: نَزَّلْنَـٰهُ تَنزِيلًا is sending it down piece by piece, which is contrasted elsewhere with the Form IV أَنزَلَ (a single sending). Same root, two causatives, different shades.

examples.translation: And Zakariyya took charge of her.
examples.note: Form I كَفَلَ = 'took on, undertook responsibility for.' Form II كَفَّلَ intensifies: took full charge of, became guardian over. The ـها on the end is 'her' (Mary).

examples.translation: The teacher instructed the students.
examples.note: Form I دَرَسَ = 'he studied.' Form II دَرَّسَ = 'he caused [others] to study' = taught. Pure causative.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs with a long ا (alif) inserted after the first root letter. The shape is fa-a-3a-la (CaaCaCa).
skills.semantic_note: Form III directs the action outward — at someone or something else. With some roots this is reciprocal (the action is mutual): كَاتَبَ = 'corresponded with,' قَاتَلَ = 'fought with.' With others it's directed at a recipient: بَارَكَ = 'bestowed blessing on.' The common thread is that Form III always has someone or something on the receiving end of the action.
skills.why_explanation: Form III adds a long ا between the first and second root letters. Semantically, this turns the verb outward — toward a partner, an opponent, or a recipient. The clearest cases are the truly reciprocal ones, where two parties are doing the action with each other: كَاتَبَ (corresponded), قَاتَلَ (fought), شَاوَرَ (consulted). With other roots the second party is just a recipient rather than a co-doer (بَارَكَ — Allah blessed the land), but the verb shape still carries the sense of action-aimed-at-someone. Form III contrasts cleanly with Form I, which doesn't specify who's on the receiving end.

examples.text: وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا
examples.translation: And We delivered him and Lot to the land that We had blessed.
examples.note: Form I root sense relates to kneeling/settling. Form III بارَك = bestowed blessing on, made blessed. The action is directed at a recipient — the land. The ـنا on the end is 'we' (Allah).

examples.text: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَهَاجَرُوا۟ وَجَـٰهَدُوا۟ بِأَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَأَنفُسِهِمْ
examples.translation: Indeed, those who believed and migrated and strove with their wealth and their lives.
examples.note: Two Form III verbs side by side. Root هجر (leaving): Form I هَجَرَ = 'abandoned'; Form III هاجَرَ = 'migrated' — left going-toward-somewhere, an outward-directed motion. Root جهد (effort): Form I جَهَدَ = 'exerted'; Form III جاهَدَ = 'struggled with/against' — directed-at-an-opposing-force effort.

examples.translation: And those who gave shelter and helped.
examples.note: Counter-example for contrast: نَصَرُوا is Form I, not Form III. Form III would be نَاصَرُوا (with a long ا after the ن) — meaning roughly 'helped one another, supported each other.' Form I نَصَرَ is the bare 'helped.' Useful to see the two side by side.

examples.translation: The father met his son after the journey.
examples.note: Form I قَبِلَ = 'accepted.' Form III قابَلَ = 'met, faced, encountered' — engaged with another person face-to-face. The directed-at-recipient quality is sharp here.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs that begin with an أ (hamza-on-alif) and have a sukun on the first root letter.
skills.semantic_note: Causative — making someone or something do or undergo the action of the root. نَزَلَ = 'came down' → أَنزَلَ = 'sent down' (caused to come down). كَرُمَ = 'was honorable' → أَكْرَمَ = 'honored' (caused to be honored).
skills.why_explanation: Form IV is the second of the two main causative forms (alongside Form II). It's built by prefixing an أ and putting a sukun on the first root letter. Forms II and IV are often interchangeable, but they tend to have subtly different feels: Form II hints at gradual, intensive, or repeated causing (نَزَّلَ — sent down piece by piece); Form IV hints at a single completed act (أَنزَلَ — sent it down once). Some roots use only Form II for the causative, some only Form IV, some both. Which a root prefers is something you absorb from exposure rather than from a rule.

examples.translation: Indeed, We sent it down on the Night of Decree.
examples.note: Form I نَزَلَ = 'came down.' Form IV أَنزَلَ = 'sent it down' — causative, single act. Compare with Form II نَزَّلَ in 17:106, which adds a sense of gradualness. Same root, two causatives, different flavors. The ـنا is 'we' (Allah), ـه is 'it' (the Quran).

examples.translation: And We have not sent you except as a mercy to the worlds.
examples.note: Root رسل underlies sending and messengers. Form IV here is the causative act of sending: 'We sent you.' Form IV is the form most commonly used with this root in the Quran when describing the sending of prophets.

examples.translation: And honoured him and favoured him.
examples.note: Form I كَرُمَ = 'was honorable' (a stative quality). Form IV أَكْرَمَ = 'made honored, honored him' — causative of the state. Notice the verse pairs أَكْرَمَ (Form IV) with نَعَّمَ (Form II) — two causative forms in a row, both meaning roughly 'treated with honor/favor.'

examples.translation: The father sent a letter to his son.
examples.note: Same Form IV, modern usage. The father is the causer — he made the sending happen.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs starting with تَ followed by the Form II shape (with the doubled middle root letter). Combined: ta-fa-3-3a-la.
skills.semantic_note: Form V is the reflexive of Form II. Where Form II makes something happen (causative), Form V is when that same action turns inward — done to or by oneself. عَلَّمَ = 'he taught' (Form II) → تَعَلَّمَ = 'he taught himself' = learned (Form V). The تَ at the front is the reflexive marker.
skills.why_explanation: Form V sits on top of Form II. Take the Form II shape (with its doubled middle), prefix تَ, and the direction flips: the action is now happening to or by the subject. This pairing — Form II for causing, Form V for the reflexive of that causing — is one of the cleanest derivational symmetries in the language. Form II عَلَّمَ = 'made [someone] learn' = taught; Form V تَعَلَّمَ = 'made oneself learn' = learned. Form II كَبَّرَ = 'declared great'; Form V تَكَبَّرَ = 'declared self great' = became arrogant. Form V perfectives in the Quran tend to be less common than imperfectives, but the pattern is the same in either tense.

examples.text: وَمَا تَفَرَّقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَتْهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَةُ
examples.translation: And those who were given the Scripture did not become divided until after clear proof had come to them.
examples.note: Form II فَرَّقَ = 'caused separation, divided [them].' Form V تَفَرَّقَ flips the direction reflexively: 'they became divided, divided themselves up.' The doers are also the ones being affected by the action.

examples.translation: Indeed, the one who purifies himself has succeeded.
examples.note: Form II زَكَّى = 'made [someone] pure, purified [them].' Form V تَزَكَّى = 'made oneself pure, purified himself.' This root has a weak last letter (و), so the shape ends in ـى rather than a clean final consonant — the Form V pattern is still recognizable underneath.

examples.translation: He frowned and turned away.
examples.note: Form II وَلَّى = 'turned [something] away, made [it] face away.' Form V تَوَلَّى = 'turned himself away.' Same reflexive shift. Weak last letter again — the ـى ending is the trace of the root's final ي folded into the pattern.

examples.translation: The student learned the lesson.
examples.note: The cleanest illustration of the II/V pair. Form II عَلَّمَ = taught. Form V تَعَلَّمَ = learned. Same root, same doubled middle, just تَ added — and the action turns from outward (teaching) to inward (learning).

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs starting with تَ followed by the Form III shape (with the long ا after the first root letter). Combined: ta-faa-3a-la.
skills.semantic_note: Form VI is the reflexive of Form III. Where Form III directs the action at another party, Form VI makes it mutual or shared — both parties doing it together. كَاتَبَ = 'corresponded with' (Form III) → تَكَاتَبَ = 'corresponded back and forth' (Form VI, two-sided).
skills.why_explanation: Form VI sits on top of Form III the same way Form V sits on top of Form II. Take the Form III shape (long ا after the first root letter), prefix تَ, and the action becomes mutual. The reciprocal sense is sharpest in some roots — تَكَاتَبَ (corresponded back and forth), تَنَاصَرَ (helped each other), تَعَاوَنَ (cooperated). With others it softens into 'gradually, distributively' — تَدَارَكَ (caught up to, overtook gradually). Form VI perfectives in the Quran are less common than imperfectives or imperatives, but where they appear the mutual/distributed sense is consistent.

examples.text: لَّوْلَآ أَن تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ
examples.translation: Had not a favor from his Lord overtaken him.
examples.note: Form I دَرَكَ = root sense of catching/reaching. Form VI تَدَارَكَ = 'overtook gradually, caught up to' — the distributive/processive sense of Form VI rather than the strictly reciprocal one. A favor from Allah catches up with the prophet (Yunus). The ـه on the end is 'him.'

examples.translation: Then when the two groups saw each other.
examples.note: Beautiful illustration of Form VI's reciprocal meaning. Root رأي (seeing). Form VI تَرَاءَى = 'saw each other, came into mutual sight.' The subject is dual — ٱلْجَمْعَانِ ('the two groups,' Moses' people and Pharaoh's army). The Form VI shape grammatically signals that what each group does (seeing) is being done by the other to them as well.

examples.translation: The two friends corresponded for many years.
examples.note: The textbook example. Form I كَتَبَ = wrote. Form III كَاتَبَ = corresponded with [someone]. Form VI تَكَاتَبَ = corresponded back and forth, mutually. Each تَ-step takes the action one degree more toward 'with each other.'

examples.translation: The two students cooperated on the homework.
examples.note: Form III عَاوَنَ = 'helped [someone].' Form VI تَعَاوَنَ = 'helped each other, cooperated.' Mutual.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs starting with ا followed by ن, then the root letters with a sukun on the first one. Shape: in-fa-3a-la.
skills.semantic_note: Form VII expresses that the action happened to the subject without the verb naming who did it. It's similar to passive voice, but specifically conveys 'the change occurred' rather than 'someone did it.' قَطَعَ = 'he cut [it]' → اِنْقَطَعَ = 'it became cut, was severed.'
skills.why_explanation: Form VII is one of the more limited forms — many roots simply don't take it. Where it does exist, the meaning is consistently 'the action happened to the subject, with the agent not named in the verb shape.' It's close to passive voice but emphasizes the change-of-state itself. ٱنفَلَقَ = 'split apart' — the verb shape doesn't say who caused the splitting; the surrounding context does. This makes Form VII especially useful in Quranic verses describing events caused by Allah: the verb expresses the event, and the surrounding meaning carries the cause.

examples.text: فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْبَحْرَ ۖ فَٱنفَلَقَ
examples.translation: We inspired Moses: 'Strike the sea with your staff.' And it split apart.
examples.note: Root فلق (splitting). Form I فَلَقَ = 'he split [it].' Form VII ٱنفَلَقَ = 'it split apart' — the change of state, with the agent backgrounded. The verse is explicit: Allah inspired Moses to strike, and the sea split. The Form VII verb expresses the splitting-event itself; the surrounding verse names the cause.

examples.text: فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِى ٱلسَّفِينَةِ
examples.translation: So they set off, until when they had embarked on the ship.
examples.note: Root طلق (releasing, going free). Form I طَلَقَ = released. Form VII ٱنطَلَقَ = 'set off, went free' — the becoming-released-and-moving event. The ـا at the end is dual ('the two of them' — Moses and his companion).

examples.translation: The Hour has drawn near, and the moon has split.
examples.note: Root شقّ (splitting — the last two letters of the root are the same, called a 'doubled' root). Form VII ٱنشَقَّ = 'split apart.' Like ٱنفَلَقَ above, the verb shape just expresses the splitting; the cause is implicit. Notice this verse pairs two grammatically similar shapes — ٱقْتَرَبَتِ (Form VIII) and ٱنشَقَّ (Form VII) — both backgrounding the agent.

examples.translation: The pen broke.
examples.note: Form I كَسَرَ = 'he broke [it].' Form VII ٱنْكَسَرَ = 'it broke' — became broken, with the agent unnamed. Compare to English: 'The pen broke' versus 'Someone broke the pen.' Same difference.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs that start with an ا and have a ت wedged between the first and second root letters.
skills.semantic_note: Form VIII often expresses that the action happens to or affects the subject, with the cause backgrounded. When the doer is a person, this often reads as reflexive — they're doing the action to or for themselves (اعتصم = 'he took hold for himself,' اجتمع = 'they gathered'). When the subject is inanimate, the verb shape expresses the event or change of state itself (اقترب = 'drew near'), without the verb naming who brings it about.
skills.why_explanation: Most verb forms shift where the action lands. Form I is the bare verb (قَرُبَ = 'is near'). Forms II and IV add 'cause' — someone makes something happen. Form VIII goes the other direction: it foregrounds the subject undergoing the action and lets the agent stay implicit. With people as the subject, this reads as reflexive — they're doing it to themselves or for themselves. With non-people as the subject, it reads as the event happening to them — the verb describes the becoming, not who causes it. So in اقتربت الساعة, the verb shape just expresses the Hour's becoming-near; the verse's broader meaning is what makes the actual cause clear.

examples.translation: And hold firmly to the rope of Allah, all together.
examples.note: Root عصم = 'protect.' Form VIII اعتصم = 'take hold for yourself, grip on.' The action turns inward — they're protecting themselves by gripping the rope. The ـوا on the end is 'you all'.

examples.translation: The Hour has drawn near.
examples.note: Form I قَرُبَ would mean 'is near' — a state. Form VIII اقترب expresses the event of becoming near, the change of state. The verb shape itself doesn't name who brings the Hour near — that's Allah, as the surrounding meaning of the Quran makes clear. The Form VIII shape just lets the verb describe the event of approaching with the cause kept implicit.

examples.translation: Avoid much suspicion.
examples.note: Root جنب = 'side.' Form VIII اجتنب = literally 'put yourself to the side of,' meaning actively keep yourself away from. The reflexive flavor is sharp here — the doer is moving themselves.

examples.translation: The students gathered in the classroom.
examples.note: From جمع (he gathered something — Form I, transitive). Form VIII اجتمع = they gathered themselves together. The action turns back on the doer.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Verbs that begin with اِسْتَ (alif + sin + ta) before the root letters.
skills.semantic_note: Form X usually means one of two things: (1) seek or ask for the action of the base verb — غَفَرَ = 'forgave' → ٱسْتَغْفَرَ = 'sought forgiveness'; or (2) consider/judge something to have the quality of the base verb — كَبُرَ = 'was great' → ٱسْتَكْبَرَ = 'considered self great' = was arrogant.
skills.why_explanation: The است- prefix is one of the cleanest semantic markers in Arabic. When you see a verb starting with است-, the meaning will usually fit one of two patterns. The first is 'seeking': asking for the base verb's action to happen. The second — sometimes called the 'esteemative' use — is judging something to have a certain quality: کبر means 'was great,' but ٱسْتَكْبَرَ doesn't mean 'sought to be great' — it means 'considered self to be great.' If you see است- on a verb, mentally try 'seek/ask for [base meaning]' or 'consider X to be [base meaning]' and you'll usually land close.

examples.translation: He refused and was arrogant and became of the disbelievers.
examples.note: Form I كَبُرَ = 'was great' (a stative quality). Form X ٱسْتَكْبَرَ = 'considered himself great' = was arrogant. This is the esteemative sense — Iblis evaluated himself as superior to Adam, not 'sought greatness.'

examples.translation: And the Messenger sought forgiveness for them.
examples.note: Root غفر (forgive). Form I غَفَرَ = 'he forgave.' Form X ٱسْتَغْفَرَ = 'sought forgiveness' — pure 'asking for [the base meaning].' The cleanest version of the seeking sense.

examples.translation: And they rejected them, while their souls were certain of them.
examples.note: Root يقن (certainty). Form X ٱسْتَيْقَنَ = 'became certain of, sought/reached certainty about.' Their tongues denied the signs while their souls had reached complete inner certainty.

examples.translation: The worker drew water out of the well.
examples.note: Form I خَرَجَ = 'went out.' Form X ٱسْتَخْرَجَ = 'sought to make come out, extracted.' The seeking sense applied to a physical action — pulling water out.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Plurals shaped أَ-ـْ-ـَ-ا-ـ : an أ at the start, sukun on the second letter, fatha on the third, then a long ا, then the last letter. The singular is rebuilt internally — letters don't just get an ending tacked on.
skills.semantic_note: One of the most common broken-plural shapes. Used widely for short three-letter nouns.
skills.why_explanation: Arabic has two strategies for plurals. Sound plurals just add an ending: ـون for masculine humans (مسلم → مسلمون), ـات for many feminine nouns (مسلمة → مسلمات). The singular stays intact. Broken plurals don't add an ending — they rebuild the word internally, like clay reshaped into a different vessel. Most non-human plurals in Arabic are broken plurals. The pattern أَفْعَال is one of the most common shapes for short three-letter singulars: نَهْر، يَوْم، وَلَد، قَلَم all become أنهار، أيام، أولاد، أقلام. There isn't a strict rule for which short noun takes which broken-plural pattern — Arabic offers أفعال، فعول، أفعِلة، فِعال and others. Each noun has its preferred shape, and the way to learn them is exposure: once you've seen a plural a few times, it sticks. أفعال is just a high-probability default for this size and shape of word.

examples.text: لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ
examples.translation: They will have gardens beneath which rivers flow.
examples.note: Singular نَهْر (river) — three letters, short shape — fits the أفعال slot cleanly. Plural أَنْهَار.

examples.translation: [For] a limited number of days.
examples.note: Singular يَوْم → plural أَيَّام. Note the tanwin ـًا on the end — that's the indefinite accusative marker, not part of the plural pattern itself.

examples.text: لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم
examples.translation: Their wealth and their children will not avail them.
examples.note: Singular وَلَد → plural أَوْلاد. Notice the verse has two أفعال plurals back-to-back: أَمْوَال (sing. مال) and أَوْلاد. Both are short singulars taking the same broken-plural shape.

examples.translation: We spent beautiful days in the summer.
examples.note: Same أَيَّام as the Quranic example, used the same way in modern Arabic. The pattern doesn't belong to one register — it's the default plural for يوم across all Arabic.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Two nouns placed side by side. The first is bare (no ال, no tanwin). The second carries a kasra (ـِ). The relationship is 'X of Y' — the first belongs to or is defined by the second.
skills.semantic_note: This is how Arabic builds 'X of Y' phrases. The first noun is never marked with ال or tanwin even when it would be elsewhere — the connection itself makes it definite or indefinite, inheriting from the second noun.
skills.why_explanation: English builds 'X of Y' with a separate word: 'name of God,' 'house of the king.' Arabic builds it by gluing two nouns together with case marking and dropping the article from the first. اسم الله — اسم is bare, الله carries the kasra, and the connection itself does the work. The reason the first never takes ال or tanwin: that would break the binding. The first noun's definiteness comes from its connection to the second — اسم الله is automatically 'the name of Allah' because الله is definite. If the second is indefinite, the whole phrase is indefinite: اسم رجل = 'a name of a man.' Idafa chains can stretch: مالك يوم الدين = 'master of the day of the judgment' is two idafas linked, with only the final word taking ال.

examples.translation: In the name of Allah.
examples.note: اسم (name) is bare — no ال, no tanwin — and الله carries the kasra. 'Name of Allah.' The بِ at the start is a separate preposition ('in/with'); the idafa is between اسم and الله.

examples.translation: All praise is for Allah, Lord of the worlds.
examples.note: رب is bare; العالمين carries the genitive ending. 'Lord of the worlds.' The first noun in idafa always drops its ال and tanwin, even when the meaning is definite.

examples.translation: Master of the Day of Judgment.
examples.note: Two idafas chained: مالك يوم ('master of [the] day') and يوم الدين ('day of the judgment'). Only the final word carries ال — that's enough to definitize the whole chain.

examples.translation: The student's book is on the desk.
examples.note: كتاب is bare; الطالب carries the kasra. 'Book of the student.' Same structure as the Quranic examples, modern wording.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A bare three-letter verb in its present-tense shape: starts with a prefix يـ، تـ، أـ، or نـ, then the three root letters with a sukun on the first root letter and one of three possible vowels (fatha, kasra, or damma) on the middle root letter. Examples: يَكْتُبُ، يَعْلَمُ، يَنْصُرُ، يَضْرِبُ.
skills.semantic_note: This is the plain, unadorned shape of the verb when the action is ongoing, habitual, or yet to happen. No extra letters wedged in, nothing doubled, no prefixes beyond the single subject letter. The middle vowel varies from verb to verb (يَكْتُبُ vs يَعْلَمُ vs يَضْرِبُ) and you mostly memorize it per verb. The damma at the end is the default ending when nothing else is acting on the verb.
skills.why_explanation: Form I is the foundation. Every other form is built by adding letters or doubling letters around these three root letters, so getting comfortable with the bare shape makes the other forms much easier to spot. The four prefix letters (يـ for he/they-masculine, تـ for she/you/they-feminine, أـ for I, نـ for we) tell you who is doing the action. The shape itself just says 'this action is happening, ongoing, or will happen' — Arabic does not split present from future at the verb level the way English does, so context decides whether to read يَكْتُبُ as 'he writes' or 'he is writing' or 'he will write.'

examples.text: وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ
examples.translation: And with Him are the keys of the unseen; none knows them except Him. And He knows what is on the land and in the sea.
examples.note: Root علم (knowing). Form I past عَلِمَ = 'he knew.' Present-tense shape يَعْلَمُ adds the يـ prefix and shifts the vowels: ya-3lamu. No extra letters, nothing doubled — the bare shape. Here it conveys ongoing, total knowledge: He knows.

examples.text: وَيَقُولُونَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَنَا مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّآ أَحْصَىٰهَا
examples.translation: And they will say, 'Oh, woe to us! What is this book that leaves nothing small or great except that it has enumerated it?'
examples.note: Root قول (saying). Form I past قَالَ = 'he said.' Present-tense shape يَقُولُ = 'he says / is saying / will say.' The ـونَ ending makes it plural masculine: 'they say.' The verse describes a Day of Judgment scene, so context lets you read this as future: 'they will say.' Same bare shape, time read from context.

examples.translation: And Allah knows what you do.
examples.note: Root صنع (making, doing, fashioning). Form I past صَنَعَ = 'he made.' Present-tense shape with the تـ prefix and ـونَ plural ending = تَصْنَعُونَ = 'you all do/make.' The verse pairs two Form I present-tense verbs back to back — يَعْلَمُ and تَصْنَعُونَ — both bare three-letter verbs in their plain present shape, just with different prefix letters naming who is doing the action.

examples.translation: The student writes the lesson.
examples.note: Root كتب (writing). Form I past كَتَبَ = 'he wrote.' Present-tense shape يَكْتُبُ — يـ prefix, sukun on the first root letter ك, damma on the middle root letter ت, damma at the end. The damma at the end is the default ending when nothing else is acting on the verb.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Present-tense shape with a damma on the prefix letter (يُـ، تُـ، أُـ، نُـ) and a shadda doubling the middle root letter. Shape: yu-fa33i-lu. Examples: يُكَبِّرُ، يُعَلِّمُ، يُنَزِّلُ، يُسَبِّحُ.
skills.semantic_note: Two visual signals to lock onto: the damma on the prefix letter (يُـ rather than يَـ) and the shadda sitting on the middle root letter. The shadda is the heart of the form — it's the doubled middle root letter that makes Form II Form II. Meaning-wise, this shape often makes a Form I verb 'cause' or 'intensify': عَلِمَ = 'he knew' → عَلَّمَ = 'he taught' (caused to know); كَبُرَ = 'was great' → كَبَّرَ = 'declared great.'
skills.why_explanation: In Form I the middle root letter sits with one vowel and that's it. Form II takes that middle root letter and doubles it — written with a shadda. That doubling is the structural change, and the meaning shift follows from it: the action is now pushed onto something or someone else, or pushed harder. The damma on the prefix letter is the second tell — يُـ instead of يَـ. Once you train your eye on those two signals (damma on prefix + shadda on middle root letter), Form II is one of the easiest forms to recognize on sight.

examples.text: تُسَبِّحُ لَهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ ٱلسَّبْعُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَن فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَإِن مِّن شَىْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِۦ
examples.translation: The seven heavens and the earth and whatever is in them exalt Him. And there is not a thing except that it exalts Him by His praise.
examples.note: Root سبح (purity, glorifying). Form II shape يُسَبِّحُ — damma on the يـ, shadda doubling the middle root letter ب. The shape is the giveaway: yu-sab-bi-hu. Form II here intensifies and directs the action — declaring Allah's perfection, doing it actively. The verse states all of creation does this; the verb itself just expresses the act of glorification.

examples.text: كَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا فِيكُمْ رَسُولًا مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا وَيُزَكِّيكُمْ وَيُعَلِّمُكُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ
examples.translation: Just as We sent among you a messenger from yourselves reciting to you Our verses and purifying you and teaching you the Book and wisdom.
examples.note: Root علم (knowing). Form I يَعْلَمُ = 'he knows.' Form II shape يُعَلِّمُ — damma on the يـ, shadda doubling the middle root letter ل — pushes the action onto someone else: 'he causes to know' = 'he teaches.' The ـكُم at the end is the object 'you all.' The verse names Allah as the sender ('We sent'); the messenger is the means through which the teaching reaches the people.

examples.text: هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ
examples.translation: He is Allah, the Creator, the Producer, the Fashioner; to Him belong the best names. Whatever is in the heavens and earth exalts Him.
examples.note: Same Form II verb as the 17:44 example, but appearing in a different verse so you can see the same shape twice. The two visual cues — damma on the يـ and shadda on the middle root letter ب — are exactly the pattern to train your eye on. Whenever you see this combination, suspect Form II first.

examples.translation: The teacher teaches the students reading.
examples.note: Root علم (knowing). Form I يَعْلَمُ = 'he knows.' Form II يُعَلِّمُ = 'he teaches' — literally 'he causes to know.' Notice how the damma on the يـ and the shadda on the middle root letter ل turn 'knowing' into 'making someone else know.' Same root, same three letters, but the doubled middle root letter pushes the action onto a recipient.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Present-tense shape with a damma on the prefix letter (يُـ، تُـ، أُـ، نُـ), a sukun on the first root letter, and a kasra on the middle root letter. Shape: yu-f3i-lu. Examples: يُنْزِلُ، يُرْسِلُ، يُخْرِجُ، يُمْسِكُ.
skills.semantic_note: Two visual signals: the damma on the prefix letter (يُـ rather than يَـ) and the kasra on the middle root letter, with no shadda. So the difference from Form II is the missing shadda — Form II doubles the middle root letter, Form IV does not. Meaning-wise, Form IV usually means 'caused something to happen' or 'made something do the base verb': نَزَلَ = 'came down' → أَنْزَلَ = 'sent down'; خَرَجَ = 'went out' → أَخْرَجَ = 'brought out, expelled.'
skills.why_explanation: Form IV and Form II both push the action onto something else — both add a 'cause' meaning to a Form I base. The eye-test for telling them apart: shadda on the middle root letter? That's Form II. No shadda but damma on the prefix and kasra on the middle root letter? That's Form IV. The two often overlap in meaning ('cause to do X') and many roots take only one or the other. The يُـ on both is the same signal — that damma on the prefix tells you 'this verb is making something else do something' before you even read the rest of the word.

examples.text: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦ
examples.translation: And it is He who sends the winds as good tidings before His mercy.
examples.note: Root رسل (sending). Form I would be رَسَلَ (rare). Form IV shape يُرْسِلُ — damma on the يـ, sukun on ر, kasra on the middle root letter س, no shadda. The verse states the subject is Allah Himself ('He who sends'), so the Form IV 'cause to go' meaning lands directly: He makes the winds travel.

examples.text: وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِۦ نَبَاتَ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ فَأَخْرَجْنَا مِنْهُ خَضِرًا نُّخْرِجُ مِنْهُ حَبًّا مُّتَرَاكِبًا
examples.translation: And it is He who sends down rain from the sky, and We produce thereby the growth of all things. We produce from it greenery from which We produce grains arranged in layers.
examples.note: Root خرج (going out). Form I يَخْرُجُ = 'goes out.' Form IV shape نُخْرِجُ — damma on the prefix نـ (the 'we' prefix), sukun on خ, kasra on the middle root letter ر. Form IV adds 'cause to': 'we cause to go out' = 'we bring out, produce.' The verse names Allah as the producer ('We produce') — the Form IV shape carries the causing-to-go-out meaning directly.

examples.text: فَيُمْسِكُ ٱلَّتِى قَضَىٰ عَلَيْهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ وَيُرْسِلُ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى
examples.translation: Then He keeps those for which He has decreed death and releases the others for a specified term.
examples.note: Root مسك (holding, gripping). Form IV shape يُمْسِكُ — damma on the يـ, sukun on the first root letter م, kasra on the middle root letter س. Form IV here = 'holds back, keeps.' The verse pairs two Form IV verbs in the same shape — يُمْسِكُ and يُرْسِلُ — and the subject of both is Allah, named in the surrounding verse.

examples.translation: The father sends a letter to his son.
examples.note: Same root as the 7:57 example. Form IV shape يُرْسِلُ — damma on the يـ, sukun on ر, kasra on the middle root letter س, no shadda. Compare visually with Form II يُسَبِّحُ from earlier: same damma on the prefix, but Form II has the shadda that Form IV does not.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Present-tense shape starting with the standard prefix letter (يـ، تـ، أـ، نـ) with a fatha, then the first root letter with a sukun, then a ت wedged in, then the second root letter with a kasra, then the third root letter. Shape: ya-f-ta-3i-lu. Examples: يَخْتَلِفُ، يَجْتَمِعُ، يَقْتَرِبُ، يَسْتَمِعُ.
skills.semantic_note: The signature of Form VIII is the ت wedged between the first and second root letters. In the past tense it sat after a starter alif (ٱخْتَلَفَ); in the present-tense shape the alif drops away and the prefix letter takes over, but the wedged ت stays. Meaning-wise, Form VIII often expresses the action happening to or affecting the subject, with the cause kept in the background. With people as the subject this often reads as reflexive (they're doing it for themselves: يَجْتَمِعُونَ = 'they gather themselves together'). With non-people as the subject it reads as the event itself happening (يَقْتَرِبُ = 'draws near' as an event).
skills.why_explanation: The wedged ت is the visual fingerprint — once you see ت sitting between the first and second root letters, suspect Form VIII. The meaning logic carries over from the past tense: the verb foregrounds the subject undergoing the action and lets the agent stay implicit. With animate subjects this lands as reflexive; with inanimate subjects it expresses the event/change-of-state without naming who brings it about. So in يَخْتَلِفُونَ ('they differ'), the verb shape highlights the people in the state of differing — what causes the differing is left for the surrounding text to explain.

examples.text: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ يَفْصِلُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ
examples.translation: Indeed, your Lord will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that over which they used to differ.
examples.note: Root خلف (being behind, differing, succeeding). Form I خَلَفَ = 'came after.' Form VIII present-tense shape يَخْتَلِفُ — fatha on the يـ, first root letter خ with sukun, then the wedged ت with a fatha, then the second root letter ل with a kasra, then the third root letter ف. The ـونَ ending makes it plural: 'they differ.' The Form VIII shape puts the people in the state of disagreeing without saying who or what causes the disagreement.

examples.text: وَلَيُبَيِّنَنَّ لَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ
examples.translation: And He will surely make clear to you on the Day of Resurrection that over which you used to differ.
examples.note: Same Form VIII verb as 32:25, but with the تـ prefix instead of يـ — 'you all differ' rather than 'they differ.' The wedged ت between the first and second root letters stays put across both prefixes. Train your eye on that ت — it is the giveaway.

examples.translation: Who listen to speech and follow the best of it.
examples.note: Root سمع (hearing). Form I يَسْمَعُ = 'he hears.' Form VIII present-tense shape يَسْتَمِعُ — fatha on the يـ, first root letter س with sukun, wedged ت with a fatha, second root letter م with a kasra, third root letter ع. The Form VIII shape turns simple hearing into active listening — the action turning back on the subject's own attention. Do not confuse this with Form X (يَسْتَفْعِلُ): Form X has both an س and a ت as prefixes before the root, while here the س is the first root letter itself and only the ت is added.

examples.translation: The friends gather at the house.
examples.note: Root جمع (gathering). Form I جَمَعَ = 'he gathered something' (transitive — gathered objects together). Form VIII present-tense shape يَجْتَمِعُ — fatha on the يـ, first root letter ج with sukun, wedged ت with a fatha, second root letter م with a kasra, third root letter ع. The action turns back on the doers themselves: they gather themselves together.

skills.user_facing_pattern: Present-tense shape: prefix letter (يـ، تـ، أـ، نـ) with a fatha, then سْتَ (sin with sukun + ta with fatha), then the three root letters with a kasra on the middle root letter. Shape: ya-sta-f3i-lu. Examples: يَسْتَغْفِرُ، يَسْتَكْبِرُ، يَسْتَعِينُ، يَسْتَطِيعُ.
skills.semantic_note: In the past tense, Form X started with ٱسْتَ (alif + sin + ta). In the present-tense shape, the alif drops away and the prefix letter (يـ، تـ، أـ، نـ) takes its place, but the سْتَ stays — that two-letter chunk سْتَ riding right after the prefix is the signature of Form X. Meaning-wise, the same two senses carry over from the past tense: (1) seeking or asking for the base verb's action — يَسْتَغْفِرُ = 'seeks forgiveness'; (2) considering/judging something to have the quality of the base verb — يَسْتَكْبِرُ = 'considers self great' = is arrogant.
skills.why_explanation: Form X is one of the easiest forms to spot once you train on the سْتَ chunk. See يَسْتَ-, تَسْتَ-, أَسْتَ-, or نَسْتَ- followed by three root letters? That's almost always Form X. When you find one, mentally try one of two readings: 'seek/ask for [base meaning]' or 'consider X to be [base meaning].' One of them will usually fit. ٱسْتَغْفَرَ (sought forgiveness) becomes يَسْتَغْفِرُ — same meaning logic, just the present-tense shape with the prefix letter swapped in for the alif.

examples.translation: It is You we worship, and it is You we ask for help.
examples.note: Root عون (helping, aid). Form I عَانَ relates to giving aid. Form X present-tense shape with the نـ prefix (the 'we' prefix) — fatha on the نـ, then the سْتَ chunk, then the root letters. Form X here is the 'seeking' sense: 'we seek/ask for help.' The verse pairs نَعْبُدُ (Form I, plain worship) with نَسْتَعِينُ (Form X, seeking help) — same 'we' prefix, but the سْتَ on the second verb adds the asking-for sense.

examples.translation: When you were asking help of your Lord, and He answered you.
examples.note: Root غوث (rescue, aid in distress). Form X present-tense shape with the تـ prefix and ـونَ plural ending — fatha on the تـ, then the سْتَ chunk, then the root letters. Form X here is again the 'seeking' sense: 'you all seek rescue.' Same سْتَ signature as نَسْتَعِينُ above, just with a different prefix and a different root.

examples.text: وَٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ
examples.translation: And ask forgiveness of your Lord and then repent to Him. Indeed, my Lord is Merciful and Affectionate.
examples.note: Root غفر (forgiving). Form I غَفَرَ = 'he forgave.' This particular verb is in the command form ('ask forgiveness!') rather than the plain present, but it shares the exact same Form X letter pattern: سْتَ chunk + three root letters غ-ف-ر, with a kasra on the middle root letter ف. Pair this in your eye with the present-tense version يَسْتَغْفِرُ ('he seeks forgiveness') and you'll recognize them both as Form X. The 'seeking' sense is the cleanest: seek the base verb's action.

examples.translation: The worker extracts the water from the well.
examples.note: Root خرج (going out). Form I يَخْرُجُ = 'goes out.' Form IV يُخْرِجُ = 'brings out.' Form X يَسْتَخْرِجُ — present-tense shape with fatha on the يـ, then the سْتَ chunk, then the root letters with a kasra on the middle root letter ر — adds the 'seeking out' sense: actively pulls/extracts. Notice how the same root sits inside three different shapes (Form I, Form IV, Form X) and the meaning shifts each time.

skills.internal_label: Active participle, Form I (اِسْم الفَاعِل من الثلاثي)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A noun-like word built on the shape فَاعِل: three root letters, with a long ا between the first and second, and a kasra (ـِ) under the second. Shape: CāCiC. كَتَبَ (he wrote) gives كَاتِب (a writer); عَلِمَ (he knew) gives عَالِم (a knower); خَلَقَ (he created) gives خَالِق (a creator).
skills.semantic_note: This shape names the one doing the action of the verb. If the verb is كَتَبَ (he wrote), the فَاعِل-shape word كَاتِب is 'the one writing, a writer.' It turns the verb into a description of whoever is performing it — sometimes as a temporary action ('the one writing right now'), sometimes as a settled trait ('a writer by occupation'). Many of Allah's names sit on this shape: ٱلْخَالِق (the Creator), ٱلْعَالِم (the Knower), فَاطِر (Originator).
skills.why_explanation: Arabic builds words from three-letter roots by pouring them into shape-templates. The فَاعِل shape is the template for 'the one doing X.' Instead of saying 'the man who knows,' Arabic just reshapes عَلِمَ into عَالِم and you get 'a knower.' This is how Arabic names doers across the language: writer, ruler, witness, judge, creator are all built this way from their verbs. Once you see the shape — long ā after the first root letter, kasra under the second — you can read the meaning directly off the verb you already know.

examples.text: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ جَاعِلِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا أُو۟لِىٓ أَجْنِحَةٍ مَّثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۚ يَزِيدُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
examples.translation: All praise is for Allah, Originator of the heavens and the earth, who made the angels messengers having wings — two, three, or four. He adds to creation what He wills. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.
examples.note: Two فَاعِل-shape words back to back. Root فطر (originating, bringing into being from nothing): فَطَرَ → فَاطِر, the One who originates. Root جعل (placing, making something into something): جَعَلَ → جَاعِل, the One who placed/made. Both are descriptions of Allah — the verse opens with praise for Him and then names Him by what He does. Long ā after the first root letter, kasra under the second. The shape hands you the verb's meaning back as a name for the doer.

examples.translation: Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Grand, the Exalted.
examples.note: Root علم (knowing). Form I عَلِمَ = he knew. Pour the root into the فَاعِل shape and you get عَالِم = the one who knows, the Knower. Here it's a name of Allah — His knowing covers both what is hidden and what is seen. The shape itself just names the doer; the verse's words make clear whose knowing it is and how complete it is.

examples.text: إِنَّ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرَٰتِ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظَـٰتِ وَٱلذَّٰكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱلذَّٰكِرَٰتِ أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا
examples.translation: Indeed, the submitting men and submitting women, the believing men and believing women, the obedient men and obedient women, the truthful men and truthful women, the patient men and patient women, the humble men and humble women, the charitable men and charitable women, the fasting men and fasting women, the men who guard their private parts and the women who do so, and the men who remember Allah often and the women who do so — Allah has prepared for them forgiveness and a great reward.
examples.note: A long list of فَاعِل-shape words used to name kinds of people. صَدَقَ (he was truthful) → صَادِق (a truthful one); صَبَرَ (he was patient) → صَابِر (a patient one); حَفِظَ (he guarded) → حَافِظ (a guarding one); ذَكَرَ (he remembered) → ذَاكِر (a remembering one). The plural ـِينَ ending stacks 'the ones who…' on top of the shape. Notice how the verse pairs each masculine plural with its feminine counterpart — the shape is the same, the gender ending is different.

examples.translation: This writer is famous.
examples.note: Root كتب (writing). Form I كَتَبَ = he wrote. Pour the root into the فَاعِل shape: كَاتِب = a writer, the one who writes. The same template you saw above with عَالِم and فَاطِر — only the root letters change.

skills.internal_label: Active participle, Form II (اِسْم الفَاعِل من فَعَّلَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A word starting with مُ, with a doubled (shadda) middle root letter and a kasra (ـِ) under it. Shape: مُفَعِّل. From فَعَّلَ verbs: نَزَّلَ (he sent down) → مُنَزِّل (the one sending down); صَدَّقَ (he confirmed) → مُصَدِّق (the one confirming); بَشَّرَ (he gave glad tidings) → مُبَشِّر (the one giving glad tidings).
skills.semantic_note: This shape names the one performing a Form II verb — the doubled-middle, intensified-or-causative kind. Form II شَدَّدَ-style verbs often add cause or intensity to the bare meaning, and this participle names whoever is doing that intensified or causative action. So كَبَّرَ (he declared something great) gives مُكَبِّر (the one declaring greatness); عَلَّمَ (he taught) gives مُعَلِّم (a teacher).
skills.why_explanation: The pattern is consistent across all Forms II–X: the active participle starts with مُ and puts a kasra under the second root letter. For Form II specifically, the shadda on the middle root letter is the giveaway — that doubling is what marks a verb as Form II in the first place, and it's preserved in the participle. Once you see مُ at the front and a doubled middle letter with a kasra under it, you know you're looking at 'the one doing the Form II action' of that root.

examples.text: وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ
examples.translation: And We have sent down to you the Book in truth, confirming what came before it of the Scripture and as a guardian over it.
examples.note: Root صدق (truthfulness, verifying as true). Form I صَدَقَ = he was truthful. Form II صَدَّقَ = he confirmed something as true (caused-truth, intensive). Pour the Form II verb into the مُفَعِّل shape: مُصَدِّق = the one confirming as true. Here the Quran is described as مُصَدِّقًا — itself confirming the earlier scriptures. The ـًا ending is just a grammatical case marking; the shape underneath is مُصَدِّق.

examples.text: قَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مُنَزِّلُهَا عَلَيْكُمْ ۖ فَمَن يَكْفُرْ بَعْدُ مِنكُمْ فَإِنِّىٓ أُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا لَّآ أُعَذِّبُهُۥٓ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ
examples.translation: Allah said, 'Indeed, I will send it down upon you, but whoever disbelieves afterwards from among you — I will surely punish him with a punishment by which I have not punished anyone among the worlds.'
examples.note: Root نزل (going down, descending). Form I نَزَلَ = it came down. Form II نَزَّلَ = he sent it down (caused it to descend, in stages). The participle is مُنَزِّل = the one sending down. Allah is speaking in the first person here, so the doer is named directly — He is the One sending down. The ـها on the end is 'it' (referring to the table from heaven). Notice the مُ + doubled middle ز + kasra: the Form II active-participle signature.

examples.text: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ إِنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ شَـٰهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا
examples.translation: O Prophet, indeed We have sent you as a witness and a bringer of glad tidings and a warner.
examples.note: Root بشر (good news, glad tidings). Form II بَشَّرَ = he conveyed glad tidings to someone. The participle مُبَشِّر = the one conveying glad tidings. The Prophet ﷺ is being described by three role-words in a row: شَاهِد (witness — Form I shape), مُبَشِّر (Form II shape), and نَذِير (a different shape meaning warner). Side by side you can compare the simpler فَاعِل shape with the مُفَعِّل shape on the same kind of role-naming.

examples.translation: The teacher is explaining the lesson.
examples.note: Same root as عَالِم above (knowing). Form I عَلِمَ = he knew. Form II عَلَّمَ = he taught (caused someone to know — note the doubled middle). The participle مُعَلِّم = the one teaching, a teacher. Compare: عَالِم (Form I shape) is 'a knower'; مُعَلِّم (Form II shape) is 'a teacher.' Same root, two different doer-shapes for two different actions built from it.

skills.internal_label: Active participle, Form IV (اِسْم الفَاعِل من أَفْعَلَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A word starting with مُ, with a sukun on the first root letter and a kasra under the second. Shape: مُفْعِل. From أَفْعَلَ verbs: أَنْذَرَ (he warned) → مُنْذِر (a warner); أَرْسَلَ (he sent) → مُرْسِل (a sender); أَسْلَمَ (he submitted) → مُسْلِم (one who submits); آمَنَ (he believed) → مُؤْمِن (a believer).
skills.semantic_note: This shape names the one performing a Form IV verb. Form IV أَفْعَلَ verbs often add a 'bring about' flavor to the bare root: نَذِرَ (was on guard) → أَنْذَرَ (warned someone, brought the warning to them); سَلِمَ (was safe/sound) → أَسْلَمَ (committed himself, brought himself into submission). The participle names whoever does that bringing-about: مُنْذِر, مُرْسِل, مُسْلِم, مُؤْمِن.
skills.why_explanation: The Form IV signature is مُ at the front, sukun on the first root letter, kasra on the second — no shadda anywhere, which is what distinguishes it from Form II. So مُسْلِم (Form IV) versus مُسَلِّم (Form II) differ only by that middle doubling, but the meanings split: مُسْلِم is 'one who submits,' مُسَلِّم would be 'one who delivers/greets.' The Quran uses Form IV active participles constantly to name role-categories — believers, warners, messengers, doers-of-good — so this shape is one of the highest-payoff to recognize.

examples.text: وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْلَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَلِكُلِّ قَوْمٍ هَادٍ
examples.translation: And those who disbelieve say, 'Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?' You are only a warner, and for every people is a guide.
examples.note: Root نذر (warning, vowing). Form IV أَنْذَرَ = he warned someone, conveyed the warning. The participle مُنْذِر = the one warning, a warner. Here the Prophet ﷺ is described as a مُنْذِر — his role is to deliver the warning. Notice the شكل: مُ + sukun on ن + kasra under ذ. No doubling: that's the Form IV mark.

examples.text: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ
examples.translation: And of the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' but they are not believers.
examples.note: Root أمن (safety, trust, security of heart). Form IV آمَنَ = he believed, came into trust/faith. The participle مُؤْمِن = the one believing, a believer. The hamza in آمَنَ becomes a small ؤ in مُؤْمِن because of how hamza interacts with the long ā — but the underlying shape is still مُفْعِل (مُ + sukun + kasra). The بِ is just the preposition 'with/as,' and ـِينَ is the plural ending.

examples.text: أَمْرًا مِّنْ عِندِنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ
examples.translation: A command from Us. Indeed, We have been sending [messengers].
examples.note: Root رسل (sending, dispatching — same root as رَسُول 'messenger'). Form IV أَرْسَلَ = he sent (caused to go forth). The participle مُرْسِل = the one sending, a sender. Here Allah refers to Himself as the One who keeps sending — the verse names the action and the doer is the divine 'We.' The ـِينَ is plural; the underlying shape is مُرْسِل.

examples.text: هُوَ مُسْلِمٌ يُصَلِّي خَمْسَ مَرَّاتٍ في اليَوْمِ.
examples.translation: He is a Muslim who prays five times a day.
examples.note: Root سلم (peace, soundness, surrender). Form IV أَسْلَمَ = he submitted, gave himself over. The participle مُسْلِم = the one submitting, a Muslim. Same shape as مُؤْمِن and مُنْذِر above: مُ at the start, sukun on the first root letter, kasra under the second. Once you see the شكل, you can read 'doer of the Form IV action' off any root.

skills.internal_label: Active participle, Form VIII (اِسْم الفَاعِل من اِفْتَعَلَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A word starting with مُ, with a ت wedged after the first root letter, and a kasra (ـِ) under the second root letter. Shape: مُفْتَعِل. From اِفْتَعَلَ verbs: اِجْتَمَعَ (they gathered) → مُجْتَمِع (one gathering); اِخْتَلَفَ (it differed) → مُخْتَلِف (differing, varied); اِقْتَرَبَ (it drew near) → مُقْتَرِب (drawing near).
skills.semantic_note: This shape names whoever (or whatever) is in the state or action expressed by a Form VIII verb. With people, Form VIII often has a reflexive flavor — 'doing it to or for themselves' — and the participle names them as such: مُجْتَمِع = one gathering themselves with others. With non-people, Form VIII expresses the event happening to the subject, and the participle names the subject in that state: مُخْتَلِف = differing (showing variety). The ت after the first root letter is the unmistakable Form VIII signature.
skills.why_explanation: Form VIII is the verb shape that backgrounds the agent and foregrounds what's happening to the subject — اِجْتَمَعَ (they gathered themselves), اِخْتَلَفَ (it came out differing), اِقْتَرَبَ (it drew near). The participle preserves that flavor while turning the verb into a describing-word. So مُخْتَلِف doesn't mean 'one who differs from someone else as an act'; it means 'in a state of variation, varied.' Reading these participles correctly means hearing the Form VIII flavor underneath: not 'the doer to others' but 'the one in this state' or 'the one doing this for themselves.'

examples.translation: And it was said to the people, 'Will you assemble?'
examples.note: Root جمع (gathering). Form I جَمَعَ = he gathered something. Form VIII اِجْتَمَعَ = they gathered themselves together (the action turns back on the doers — they assembled). The participle مُجْتَمِع = one gathering themselves with others, an assembler. Plural here with ـُونَ. Notice the شكل: مُ + first root letter ج with sukun + ت + kasra under م + last root letter ع. The wedged ت is the Form VIII fingerprint.

examples.text: وَمَا ذَرَأَ لَكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَٰنُهُۥٓ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَذَّكَّرُونَ
examples.translation: And [in] what He has multiplied for you on the earth of varying colors. Indeed in that is a sign for a people who remember.
examples.note: Root خلف (coming after, differing, succession). Form VIII اِخْتَلَفَ = it came out varied, differed. The participle مُخْتَلِف = varied, in a state of difference. Here it describes what Allah multiplied on the earth — things مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَانُهُ, 'with their colors varied.' The participle is doing what Form VIII does generally: describing the state of variation without naming who causes it. The verse itself names the cause — Allah is the one who multiplied them.

examples.text: وَلَوْ شَآءَ رَبُّكَ لَجَعَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً ۖ وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ
examples.translation: And if your Lord had willed, He would have made mankind one community; but they continue to be in disagreement.
examples.note: Same root and shape as the previous example, but applied to people now: مُخْتَلِفِين = ones in disagreement, ones differing among themselves. The plural ـِينَ is attached. The شكل is identical: مُ + خ with sukun + ت + kasra + ل + ف. Same Form VIII active-participle template, here naming people in a state of difference rather than things in a state of variation.

examples.translation: The Eid is drawing near.
examples.note: Root قرب (nearness). Form VIII اِقْتَرَبَ = it drew near (the event of becoming-near). The participle مُقْتَرِب = drawing near, in the state of approach. Same template as مُجْتَمِع and مُخْتَلِف: مُ at the front, ت wedged after the first root letter, kasra under the second. The Form VIII flavor — describing the state of approach rather than naming who brings it about — carries straight into the participle.

skills.internal_label: Passive participle, Form I (اِسْم المَفْعُول من الثلاثي)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A word on the shape مَفْعُول: مَ at the front, sukun on the first root letter, then a long و between the second and third root letters. Shape: maCCūC. كَتَبَ (he wrote) → مَكْتُوب (something written); عَلِمَ (he knew) → مَعْلُوم (something known); حَفِظَ (he guarded) → مَحْفُوظ (something guarded, preserved); رَحِمَ (he had mercy) → مَرْحُوم (one shown mercy).
skills.semantic_note: This shape names whoever or whatever the verb's action has been done to. The فَاعِل shape names the doer; the مَفْعُول shape names the receiver. كَاتِب is the writer; مَكْتُوب is the thing written. عَالِم is the knower; مَعْلُوم is the thing known. The shape carries a finished, done-to flavor — the action has landed on this thing.
skills.why_explanation: Once you have the doer-shape فَاعِل clear, the receiver-shape مَفْعُول is its mirror. The same verb gives you both: from كَتَبَ you get كَاتِب (writer) and مَكْتُوب (written thing); from حَفِظَ you get حَافِظ (preserver) and مَحْفُوظ (preserved thing). Recognizing مَفْعُول is mostly about spotting the مَ at the start and the long و before the last root letter. Many Quranic phrases describe things in this shape — مَحْفُوظ in a preserved tablet, مَكْنُون hidden away, مَشْهُود witnessed — and reading the shape directly tells you 'this is the thing X has been done to.'

examples.translation: In a Preserved Tablet.
examples.note: Root حفظ (guarding, preserving). Form I حَفِظَ = he guarded/preserved. The doer-shape would be حَافِظ (a preserver). The receiver-shape is مَحْفُوظ = preserved, the thing being guarded. Here it describes the لَوْح (tablet) on which the Quran exists — preserved by Allah from any change. The شكل: مَ + ح with sukun + ف + long و + ظ. The long و before the last root letter is the مَفْعُول signature.

examples.translation: In a Book well-protected.
examples.note: Root كنن (concealing, sheltering — the last two letters of the root are the same, a 'doubled' root). Form I كَنَّ = he concealed/sheltered. The receiver-shape مَكْنُون = something kept concealed, hidden away. Same template as مَحْفُوظ: مَ + first root letter with sukun + long و before the last. Even with a doubled root, the shape comes through clearly.

examples.text: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّمَنْ خَافَ عَذَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّجْمُوعٌ لَّهُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَّشْهُودٌ
examples.translation: Indeed in that is a sign for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a Day for which the people will be gathered, and that is a Day witnessed.
examples.note: Two مَفْعُول-shape words side by side. Root جمع (gathering): جَمَعَ (he gathered) → مَجْمُوع (gathered, assembled). Root شهد (witnessing): شَهِدَ (he witnessed) → مَشْهُود (witnessed, attended). The Day of Judgment is described as the day for which people will be gathered, and as a witnessed Day — both shapes describing the day as the receiver of these actions, with the agent unnamed in the shape itself. Compare to شَاهِد (witness, the doer-shape) versus مَشْهُود (the witnessed thing, the receiver-shape) — same root, mirror shapes.

examples.translation: This book is written in beautiful handwriting.
examples.note: Root كتب (writing). Form I كَتَبَ = he wrote. The doer-shape كَاتِب is a writer; the receiver-shape مَكْتُوب is the thing written. Same root, two mirror shapes — one names who does, one names what is done to. مَ at the start, sukun on ك, long و between ت and ب: the مَفْعُول template carries straight across from Quranic to modern Arabic.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A noun starting with تَ, then the first root letter with sukun, then the second root letter with kasra and a long ي, then the third root letter. Shape: taf-3eel.
skills.semantic_note: This is the action-as-a-thing word that pairs with Form II verbs (the فَعَّلَ shape). كَبَّرَ is the verb 'declared greatness'; تَكْبِير is the same action turned into a noun — 'the act of declaring greatness.' Arabic builds these noun-shapes for almost every verb shape, so once you know the verb you can almost always predict its action-noun.
skills.why_explanation: English handles 'the action as a thing' with two patterns: '-ing' words (teaching, sending) and Latin nouns (instruction, transmission). Arabic does it with a small set of dedicated noun-shapes, one for each verb shape. Form II verbs (فَعَّلَ — the doubled-middle shape that often means 'cause to be' or 'do intensively') pair with the تَفْعِيل noun-shape. So عَلَّمَ ('he taught') → تَعْلِيم ('teaching, the act of teaching'); نَزَّلَ ('he sent down') → تَنْزِيل ('sending-down'). Once you can recognize تَفْعِيل by its shape, you can read the meaning straight off the root.

examples.text: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ تَنزِيلًا
examples.translation: Indeed, We have sent down to you the Qur'an, a [progressive] sending-down.
examples.note: Root نزل (coming down). Form II نَزَّلَ = 'sent down,' often with the flavor of sending in stages. تَنْزِيل is the same action turned into a noun — 'the sending-down itself.' The verse uses the verb نَزَّلْنَا and then echoes it with the matching noun تَنْزِيلًا — a common Arabic move that intensifies the action. Allah is the speaker and the sender; the noun-shape simply names the act.

examples.text: إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيُذْهِبَ عَنكُمُ ٱلرِّجْسَ أَهْلَ ٱلْبَيْتِ وَيُطَهِّرَكُمْ تَطْهِيرًا
examples.translation: Allah only wants to remove impurity from you, O people of the household, and to purify you with a [thorough] purification.
examples.note: Root طهر (purity). Form II طَهَّرَ = 'purified [thoroughly].' تَطْهِير is that same act as a noun — 'the act of purifying.' The verse pairs the verb يُطَهِّرَ with the noun تَطْهِيرًا right after it, intensifying the action. Allah is the one purifying; the noun simply names the deed.

examples.translation: And recite the Qur'an with measured recitation.
examples.note: Root رتل (arranging in good order). Form II رَتَّلَ = 'recited with careful, measured pacing.' تَرْتِيل is that act as a noun — 'measured recitation.' Same pattern as above: the verb رَتِّلِ followed by the matching noun تَرْتِيلًا, which strengthens the command.

examples.translation: Teaching Arabic requires patience.
examples.note: From عَلَّمَ ('he taught' — Form II). تَعْلِيم is the act of teaching turned into a noun, working here as the subject of the sentence. Same shape (تَفْعِيل) as تَنْزِيل and تَطْهِير above.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A noun starting with إِ (alif with kasra), then the first root letter with sukun, then the second root letter with fatha, then a long ا, then the third root letter. Shape: if-3aal.
skills.semantic_note: This is the action-as-a-thing word that pairs with Form IV verbs (the أَفْعَلَ shape, which usually means 'cause to do/be'). أَسْلَمَ is the verb 'submitted'; إِسْلَام is that same act as a noun — 'submission, the act of giving oneself over.' آمَنَ ('believed') pairs with إِيمَان ('faith, the act of believing').
skills.why_explanation: Form IV verbs typically take an outside object and cause something to happen to it: أَنْزَلَ = 'he sent [it] down,' أَرْسَلَ = 'he sent [it].' Their action-noun shape is إِفْعَال — the same root letters dressed in a different vowel pattern, with a long ا before the last letter. So نزل + Form IV verb أَنْزَلَ pairs with the noun إِنْزَال; سلم + Form IV verb أَسْلَمَ pairs with إِسْلَام. The noun lets you talk about the doing without having to put it in a sentence as a verb. إِيمَان is built from آمَنَ the same way (the hamza-alif at the start hides the same pattern), which is why faith in Arabic is literally 'the act of believing.'

examples.translation: Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam (submission).
examples.note: Root سلم (peace, wholeness, surrender). Form IV verb أَسْلَمَ = 'he submitted, gave himself over.' إِسْلَام is the same act as a noun — 'submission, the act of giving oneself over [to Allah].' The verse names this act-as-a-thing as the religion accepted by Allah.

examples.translation: And it increased them only in faith and submission.
examples.note: Root أمن (safety, trust). Form IV verb آمَنَ = 'he believed, entrusted himself.' إِيمَان is that act turned into a noun — 'faith, the act of believing.' The shape is إِفْعَال like إِسْلَام — the opening hamza just blends with the root's hamza so it looks slightly different on the surface. Notice the verse pairs إِيمَان (Form IV noun) with تَسْلِيم (Form II noun) — two different shapes side by side.

examples.translation: But Allah has endeared faith to you.
examples.note: Same noun إِيمَان as above, here as the direct object of the sentence. The verse credits Allah with making faith beloved to the believers — the noun-shape simply names the thing made beloved, while the verb حَبَّبَ names the doing and Allah is the doer.

examples.translation: Sending messages is easy today.
examples.note: From أَرْسَلَ ('he sent' — Form IV). إِرْسَال is the act of sending as a noun, working here as the subject. Same shape (إِفْعَال) as إِسْلَام and إِيمَان above — alif-kasra at the start, long ا before the final root letter.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A noun starting with اِ (alif with kasra), then the first root letter with sukun, then ت with kasra, then the second root letter with fatha, then a long ا, then the third root letter. Shape: if-ti-3aal.
skills.semantic_note: This is the action-as-a-thing word that pairs with Form VIII verbs (the اِفْتَعَلَ shape, where a ت is wedged between the first and second root letters). اِخْتَلَفَ is the verb 'differed'; اِخْتِلَاف is that act as a noun — 'differing, difference.' اِبْتَغَى ('sought') pairs with اِبْتِغَاء ('the seeking').
skills.why_explanation: Every verb shape has a paired noun-shape, and Form VIII's pairing is one of the most predictable. Take the verb اِفْتَعَلَ, swap the second fatha for kasra, stretch the third vowel into a long ا, and you get اِفْتِعَال. The little ت from the verb stays right where it was. So اِخْتَلَفَ → اِخْتِلَاف, اِجْتَمَعَ → اِجْتِمَاع, اِبْتَغَى → اِبْتِغَاء. Whenever you see a noun starting with ا and showing this ت-after-the-first-root-letter pattern with the long ا near the end, it's a Form VIII action-noun.

examples.text: وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوَٰنِكُمْ
examples.translation: And among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth, and the variation of your tongues and colors.
examples.note: Root خلف (coming after, differing). Form VIII verb اِخْتَلَفَ = 'differed, varied.' اِخْتِلَاف is that act as a noun — 'differing, variation.' The verse lists Allah's signs and names this variation among them; the noun-shape simply names the phenomenon, and the verse's broader meaning makes clear that Allah is its source.

examples.text: إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ
examples.translation: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of night and day...
examples.note: Same noun اِخْتِلَاف as above, here meaning 'alternation' — night and day taking turns one after the other. Form VIII has both senses (varying, taking turns) because the underlying root خلف covers them. Again the verse names this as a sign; the noun captures the phenomenon while Allah is the cause.

examples.translation: Except seeking the countenance of his Lord, the Most High.
examples.note: Root بغي (seeking). Form VIII verb اِبْتَغَى = 'he sought.' اِبْتِغَاء is that same act as a noun — 'the seeking.' The final hamza is what the long ا turns into when the root ends in a weak letter (و or ي), but the shape is still recognizably اِفْتِعَال — alif at the start, ت after the first root letter, long vowel before the end.

examples.translation: The family gathering was lovely.
examples.note: From اِجْتَمَعَ ('they gathered' — Form VIII). اِجْتِمَاع is the gathering as a noun, working here as the subject. Same shape (اِفْتِعَال) as اِخْتِلَاف and اِبْتِغَاء above.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A noun starting with اِسْتِ (alif-kasra, sin-sukun, ta-kasra), then the first root letter with sukun, then the second root letter with fatha, then a long ا, then the third root letter. Shape: is-tif-3aal.
skills.semantic_note: This is the action-as-a-thing word that pairs with Form X verbs (the اِسْتَفْعَلَ shape, which usually means 'seek/ask for' or 'consider something to be'). اِسْتَغْفَرَ is the verb 'sought forgiveness'; اِسْتِغْفَار is the same act as a noun — 'the seeking of forgiveness.' اِسْتَكْبَرَ ('considered self great, was arrogant') pairs with اِسْتِكْبَار ('arrogance, the acting-arrogant').
skills.why_explanation: Form X is the verb shape with the recognizable است- prefix, and its noun-shape keeps the same prefix and just rearranges the vowels around the root: اِسْتِفْعَال. So اِسْتَغْفَرَ → اِسْتِغْفَار, اِسْتَكْبَرَ → اِسْتِكْبَار, اِسْتَخْرَجَ → اِسْتِخْرَاج. The meaning carries straight through from the verb: if the verb is 'seek forgiveness,' the noun is 'the seeking of forgiveness.' Once you can spot است- at the front and the long ا near the end, the meaning falls out of the root.

examples.text: وَمَا كَانَ ٱسْتِغْفَارُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَن مَّوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَهَآ إِيَّاهُ
examples.translation: And the seeking of forgiveness by Abraham for his father was only because of a promise he had made to him.
examples.note: Root غفر (forgiveness, covering over). Form X verb اِسْتَغْفَرَ = 'he sought forgiveness.' اِسْتِغْفَار is that act as a noun — 'the seeking of forgiveness.' The noun is the subject of the sentence here, and Abraham (إِبْرَاهِيم) is the one doing the seeking, expressed by the noun's possessive attachment.

examples.translation: Out of arrogance in the land and the plotting of evil.
examples.note: Root كبر (greatness). Form X verb اِسْتَكْبَرَ = 'he considered himself great, was arrogant.' اِسْتِكْبَار is the same as a noun — 'arrogance, the acting-arrogant.' Same shape (اِسْتِفْعَال) as اِسْتِغْفَار.

examples.translation: And they persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance.
examples.note: Same noun اِسْتِكْبَار. Notice the construction: the verb اِسْتَكْبَرُوا is followed immediately by its matching noun اِسْتِكْبَارًا, which intensifies the action — a common Arabic move ('they were arrogant with great arrogance'). The verb-noun pairing makes the shape relationship visible side by side.

examples.translation: Extracting oil is expensive.
examples.note: From اِسْتَخْرَجَ ('he extracted, drew out' — Form X). اِسْتِخْرَاج is the act of extracting as a noun, working here as the subject. Same shape (اِسْتِفْعَال) as اِسْتِغْفَار and اِسْتِكْبَار above.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A many-of-something word with the shape: first root letter with damma, second root letter with damma and a long و, then the third root letter. Shape: fu-3uul.
skills.semantic_note: This is one of the most common shapes Arabic uses to mean 'more than two of these.' The singular and the many-form share the same three root letters but the vowels rearrange completely: قَلْب (one heart) → قُلُوب (hearts), بَيْت (one house) → بُيُوت (houses), نَجْم (one star) → نُجُوم (stars). You don't add an ending — you reshape the word from the inside.
skills.why_explanation: Arabic has two systems for many-of-something. The first just sticks an ending on the singular and is predictable. The second — this one — pours the root letters into a brand new mold. فُعُول is one of the workhorse molds in that second system, and the Quran is full of it: قُلُوب (hearts), بُيُوت (houses), عُيُون (eyes/springs), نُجُوم (stars), جُنُود (troops), شُهُور (months), دُرُوس (lessons). Recognizing the shape — two dammas and a long و wedged into a three-letter root — lets you read 'many of [X]' straight off the page even if you've never seen that exact word before. Many فُعُول words are non-human (parts of the body, objects, abstract things), which fits a broader Arabic pattern: non-human plurals often get treated as a single feminine bundle in the grammar of the surrounding sentence.

examples.text: ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَهِىَ كَٱلْحِجَارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْحِجَارَةِ لَمَا يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَشَّقَّقُ فَيَخْرُجُ مِنْهُ ٱلْمَآءُ ۚ وَإِنَّ مِنْهَا لَمَا يَهْبِطُ مِنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ
examples.translation: Then your hearts hardened after that, becoming like stones or even harder. For indeed, there are stones from which rivers burst forth, and others which split open and water comes out, and others which fall down out of fear of Allah. And Allah is not unaware of what you do.
examples.note: Root قلب (turning, the heart). Singular قَلْب (heart) → many-form قُلُوب (hearts), with the كُم on the end meaning 'your.' The vowels are reshuffled into the fu-3uul mold: ق with damma, ل with damma and a long و, then ب.

examples.text: أَفَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَتَكُونَ لَهُمْ قُلُوبٌ يَعْقِلُونَ بِهَآ أَوْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۖ فَإِنَّهَا لَا تَعْمَى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ وَلَـٰكِن تَعْمَى ٱلْقُلُوبُ ٱلَّتِى فِى ٱلصُّدُورِ
examples.translation: Have they not traveled through the land so that their hearts may understand and their ears may hear? For indeed, it is not the eyes that grow blind, but it is the hearts in the chests that grow blind.
examples.note: Root صدر (chest, front). Singular صَدْر (chest) → many-form صُدُور (chests). Same shape as قُلُوب — first root letter with damma, second root letter with damma and a long و, then the third. The verse stacks several فُعُول words in a row: قُلُوب (hearts) and صُدُور (chests).

examples.translation: By the star when it descends.
examples.note: Here the singular نَجْم (star) is what appears in the verse — included to anchor the contrast. The many-form is نُجُوم (stars), built by pouring these same three root letters into the fu-3uul mold: نُجُوم. The Quran uses نُجُوم elsewhere (e.g., 6:97, 16:12) for the stars by which people find their way.

examples.translation: The houses in this city are old.
examples.note: Singular بَيْت (house) → many-form بُيُوت (houses). Same fu-3uul mold as قُلُوب and صُدُور and نُجُوم: the three root letters with damma, then damma and long و, then the last letter.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A many-of-something word with the shape: first root letter with kasra, second root letter with fatha and a long ا, then the third root letter. Shape: fi-3aal.
skills.semantic_note: Another very common reshape-from-the-inside many-form. The signal is a kasra on the first letter and a long ا between the second and third: جَبَل (one mountain) → جِبَال (mountains), رَجُل (one man) → رِجَال (men), كَبِير (big, of one thing) → كِبَار (big, of many things).
skills.why_explanation: فِعَال pairs with several different singular shapes — رَجُل, جَبَل, كَبِير, جَمَل all take it — so you can't always predict it from the singular alone, but you can always recognize it on sight by the kasra-then-long-ا profile. It's used for both human plurals (رِجَال 'men') and non-human plurals (جِبَال 'mountains,' بِحَار 'seas,' رِيَاح 'winds,' جِمَال 'camels'). When you see a word with this shape in a Quranic verse, you're almost certainly looking at 'many of [X],' even if it's a noun you've never met. Note that with adjectives like كَبِير → كِبَار ('big ones'), Arabic uses the same many-form shape: the adjective changes its mold to match a many-noun.

examples.translation: And the mountains He set firm.
examples.note: Root جبل (mountain). Singular جَبَل (one mountain) → many-form جِبَال (mountains). Kasra on ج, fatha and long ا between ب and ل: ji-baal. The verse describes Allah firmly setting them; the noun-shape itself just names 'many mountains.'

examples.text: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا
examples.translation: O mankind, be mindful of your Lord who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women...
