examples.note: Root رجل (man, foot). Singular رَجُل (one man) → many-form رِجَال (men). Same fi-3aal mold as جِبَال. Note also that وَنِسَآءً here is a different many-form (the irregular plural of اِمْرَأَة 'woman').

examples.text: وَٱلْأَرْضَ مَدَدْنَـٰهَا وَأَلْقَيْنَا فِيهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مَّوْزُونٍ
examples.translation: And the earth We have spread out, and cast therein firmly set [mountains], and caused to grow therein every well-balanced thing.
examples.note: Included as a contrast — رَوَاسِي is a different many-form shape (فَوَاعِل) used for 'firmly anchored mountains.' Compare to the fi-3aal shape جِبَال 'mountains' from the previous verse: same meaning, different mold. Arabic often has more than one many-form available for the same idea, and the Quran uses both.

examples.translation: The older men are sitting in the cafe.
examples.note: Singular كَبِير (big, old) → many-form كِبَار. Same fi-3aal mold: kasra on ك, fatha and long ا between ب and ر. Both الرِّجَالُ and الكِبَارُ in this sentence sit in the same mold — the adjective shifts to match the noun's many-form.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A many-of-something word starting with أَ, then the first root letter with sukun, then the second root letter with kasra, then the third root letter, then ة. Shape: af-3i-la.
skills.semantic_note: This many-form is built by adding an أَ at the front and a ة at the end, with a kasra slipped into the middle. أَجْنِحَة (wings) from جَنَاح (one wing). أَفْئِدَة (hearts) from فُؤَاد (one heart). أَلْسِنَة (tongues) from لِسَان (one tongue). The shape is easy to spot: it begins with أ and ends with ة.
skills.why_explanation: The opening أ and closing ة are the visible markers. Between them sit the three root letters in af-3i-la order. This shape is mostly used for non-human things — body parts you can have several of (tongues, hearts, wings), and objects (weapons, clothes, foods, times). Because Arabic groups non-human many-forms grammatically as a single feminine bundle, you'll often see verbs and adjectives around these words taking feminine-singular shapes even though the meaning is plural. Spotting the أ...ة frame is enough to recognize the pattern; you don't need to know the singular to know you're looking at 'many.'

examples.text: ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ جَاعِلِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا أُو۟لِىٓ أَجْنِحَةٍ مَّثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۚ يَزِيدُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
examples.translation: All praise is to Allah, Originator of the heavens and the earth, who made the angels messengers having wings — two, three, or four. He adds to creation what He wills. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent.
examples.note: Root جنح (wing, side). Singular جَنَاح (one wing) → many-form أَجْنِحَة (wings). The shape: أَ + ج with sukun + ن with kasra + ح + ة. Same af-3i-la frame as أَفْئِدَة and أَلْسِنَة below.

examples.text: رَّبَّنَآ إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى بِوَادٍ غَيْرِ ذِى زَرْعٍ عِندَ بَيْتِكَ ٱلْمُحَرَّمِ رَبَّنَا لِيُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ فَٱجْعَلْ أَفْـِٔدَةً مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ تَهْوِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ وَٱرْزُقْهُم مِّنَ ٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْكُرُونَ
examples.translation: Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants in an uncultivated valley near Your sacred House, our Lord, that they may establish prayer. So make hearts among the people incline toward them and provide them with fruits, that they may be grateful.
examples.note: Root فأد (the inner heart). Singular فُؤَاد (one heart) → many-form أَفْئِدَة (hearts). Same af-3i-la frame: أ at the front, ة at the end, the three root letters in between. Note Arabic has two different words it translates as 'hearts' — قُلُوب (from قَلْب, fu-3uul mold) and أَفْئِدَة (from فُؤَاد, af-3i-la mold). They overlap in meaning but live in different molds.

examples.text: وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَأَلْوَٰنِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّلْعَـٰلِمِينَ
examples.translation: And of His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and the diversity of your tongues and colors. Indeed in that are signs for those of knowledge.
examples.note: Root لسن (tongue, language). Singular لِسَان (one tongue/language) → many-form أَلْسِنَة (tongues/languages). The كُم on the end means 'your.' Same af-3i-la frame: أ at the front, ة at the end (here written as ت because something is attached after it), root letters in between.

examples.translation: The restaurant's foods are delicious.
examples.note: Singular طَعَام (one food) → many-form أَطْعِمَة (foods, types of food). Same af-3i-la frame as أَجْنِحَة, أَفْئِدَة, أَلْسِنَة: أ at the front, ة at the end, root letters in between.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A many-of-something word with the shape: first root letter with damma, second root letter with fatha, third root letter with fatha and a long ا, then a final hamza. Shape: fu-3a-laa'.
skills.semantic_note: This many-form is reserved almost exclusively for groups of people, especially when the singular is a describing-word about a person: شَهِيد (one witness/martyr) → شُهَدَاء (witnesses), عَالِم (one knower/scholar) → عُلَمَاء (scholars), فَقِير (one poor person) → فُقَرَاء (poor people), وَزِير (one minister) → وُزَرَاء (ministers).
skills.why_explanation: Many singulars on the شَكْل of فَعِيل (like كَرِيم, شَهِيد, فَقِير, عَلِيم) and on the شَكْل of فَاعِل (like عَالِم, شَاعِر) take this shape when they describe a group of human beings. The signal is the closing -aa' (a long ا followed by a hamza), which gives these plurals a distinctive ending sound. When you spot a word ending in ـَاء after two reshuffled root letters, and the surrounding text is talking about people, you're almost certainly looking at this human-many-form. The Quran uses these words constantly: عُلَمَاء (those with knowledge), شُهَدَاء (witnesses), فُقَرَاء (the poor), شُرَكَاء (partners associated with Allah), بُلَغَاء, خُلَفَاء, أُمَنَاء.

examples.text: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَٱلدَّوَآبِّ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَٰنُهُۥ كَذَٰلِكَ ۗ إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى ٱللَّهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ ٱلْعُلَمَـٰٓؤُا۟ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ غَفُورٌ
examples.translation: And among people and beasts and grazing animals are various colors similarly. Only those of His servants who have knowledge are truly in awe of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Mighty and Forgiving.
examples.note: Root علم (knowing). Singular عَالِم (one knower, one with knowledge) → many-form عُلَمَاء (those with knowledge). The fu-3a-laa' mold: damma on ع, fatha on ل, fatha and long ا between م and the closing hamza. The shape itself signals this is a group of people.

examples.text: وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ وَٱدْعُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ
examples.translation: And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down upon Our Servant, then produce a chapter the like thereof and call upon your witnesses other than Allah, if you should be truthful.
examples.note: Root شهد (witnessing). Singular شَهِيد (one witness) → many-form شُهَدَاء (witnesses). The كُم on the end means 'your.' Same fu-3a-laa' mold as عُلَمَاء — damma, fatha, then long ا and hamza at the end.

examples.text: ۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ أَنتُمُ ٱلْفُقَرَآءُ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ
examples.translation: O mankind, it is you who are in need of Allah, while Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy.
examples.note: Root فقر (need, poverty). Singular فَقِير (one in need) → many-form فُقَرَاء (those in need). Same fu-3a-laa' mold as عُلَمَاء and شُهَدَاء. The verse addresses humanity as a whole, and the human-many-form shape itself signals that.

examples.translation: The ministers are meeting today.
examples.note: Singular وَزِير (one minister) → many-form وُزَرَاء (ministers). Same fu-3a-laa' mold: damma, fatha, fatha plus long ا, hamza. Used here for a group of human officeholders — exactly the kind of meaning this mold is reserved for.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A word with the singular intact and one of two endings tacked on: ـُونَ or ـِينَ. The choice between the two depends on the role the word plays in the sentence.
skills.semantic_note: Unlike the reshape-from-the-inside many-forms, this one just adds an ending to the singular. مُسْلِم (one who submits) → مُسْلِمُونَ or مُسْلِمِينَ (those who submit). مُؤْمِن (one who believes) → مُؤْمِنُونَ or مُؤْمِنِينَ. The two endings carry the same many-meaning; which one shows up depends on whether the word is the doer of the verb or something else in the sentence.
skills.why_explanation: This many-form is mostly used for human males or mixed groups described by an active-doing word — words shaped like مُسْلِم, مُؤْمِن, كَافِر, عَامِل, صَادِق. You add ـُونَ when the word is the subject of a verb ('the believers said...'), and ـِينَ when the word is the object, follows a preposition, or is being possessed ('to the believers...'). In the Quran the same group will appear with both endings depending on the sentence: ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ when they're acting, ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ when they're being addressed, blessed, or led. Because the singular stays whole inside the word, this many-form is one of the easier ones to read: just strip the ending and you have the singular back.

examples.text: وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَيُطِيعُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ سَيَرْحَمُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ
examples.translation: The believing men and believing women are allies of one another. They enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, and establish prayer, and give zakat, and obey Allah and His Messenger. Those — Allah will have mercy upon them. Indeed, Allah is Mighty and Wise.
examples.note: Singular مُؤْمِن (one who believes) → many-form ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (the believers). The ـُونَ ending here because they're the subject doing the verbs (يَأْمُرُونَ, يُقِيمُونَ, يُؤْتُونَ). The singular مُؤْمِن sits intact at the start of the word — only the ending is added.

examples.text: يَقُولُونَ لَئِن رَّجَعْنَآ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ لَيُخْرِجَنَّ ٱلْأَعَزُّ مِنْهَا ٱلْأَذَلَّ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِۦ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
examples.translation: They say, 'If we return to Medina, the more honored will surely expel from it the more humble.' And to Allah belongs honor, and to His Messenger, and to the believers, but the hypocrites do not know.
examples.note: Same word مُؤْمِن but here the ending is ـِينَ — because of the لِ ('to/for') in front of it. لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ = 'to the believers.' Compare with the previous example where the same group appeared as ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (subject ending). Same many-form, different role in the sentence, different ending.

examples.text: إِنَّ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰبِرَٰتِ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعِينَ وَٱلْخَـٰشِعَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقِينَ وَٱلْمُتَصَدِّقَـٰتِ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰٓئِمَـٰتِ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظِينَ فُرُوجَهُمْ وَٱلْحَـٰفِظَـٰتِ وَٱلذَّٰكِرِينَ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱلذَّٰكِرَٰتِ أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا
examples.translation: Indeed, the Muslim men and Muslim women, the believing men and believing women, the obedient men and obedient women, the truthful men and truthful women... — Allah has prepared for them forgiveness and a great reward.
examples.note: Singular مُسْلِم → many-form ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ. The ـِينَ ending because the whole long list is being predicated on by أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُم at the end (governed by إِنَّ at the start). Note the rhythmic alternation in this verse: each ـِينَ word for males is paired with a ـَات word for females (ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ / ٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ) — the two sound-many-forms working as a matched pair.

examples.translation: The engineers work at the company.
examples.note: Singular مُهَنْدِس (one engineer) → many-form المُهَنْدِسُونَ (the engineers). The ـُونَ ending because they're the subject doing the verb يَعْمَلُونَ. Strip the ending and you get the singular back unchanged — that's the signature of this many-form.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A word ending in ـَات. Often the singular ends in ة, and the ة gets replaced by ـَات to make the many-form. Sometimes the singular doesn't end in ة and the ـَات is just added on.
skills.semantic_note: The ـَات ending is one of the most visible many-form signals in Arabic. مُسْلِمَة (one female who submits) → مُسْلِمَات (females who submit). آيَة (one sign/verse) → آيَات (signs/verses). جَنَّة (one garden) → جَنَّات (gardens). سَمَاء (one heaven) → سَمَاوَات (heavens, with a small reshuffle in the middle).
skills.why_explanation: The ـَات ending does two related jobs. First, it's the standard many-form for feminine humans — مُسْلِمَات (Muslim women), مُؤْمِنَات (believing women), صَالِحَات (righteous women). Second, it's the standard many-form for many non-human nouns even when the singular doesn't end in ة — آيَات (signs), سَمَاوَات (heavens), حَسَنَات (good deeds). Because Arabic groups non-human many-forms grammatically as a single feminine bundle, the ـَات ending fits both jobs naturally. When you see ـَات at the end of a word, you're looking at 'many of [X]' — the only question is whether [X] is a group of women or a group of things. The Quran is full of paired ـِينَ / ـَات constructions (ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ وَٱلْمُسْلِمَـٰتِ) where the ـَات word is the female counterpart to a ـِينَ word.

examples.text: وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ كُلَّمَا رُزِقُوا۟ مِنْهَا مِن ثَمَرَةٍ رِّزْقًا ۙ قَالُوا۟ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى رُزِقْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَأُتُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُتَشَـٰبِهًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِيهَآ أَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ ۖ وَهُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ
examples.translation: And give good tidings to those who believe and do righteous deeds that they will have gardens beneath which rivers flow...
examples.note: Singular جَنَّة (one garden) → many-form جَنَّات (gardens). The ة of the singular gets replaced by ـَات. Same verse also has ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ — singular صَالِحَة (a good deed) → many-form ٱلصَّالِحَات (good deeds). Two ـَات words in one verse, both naming many of a thing.

examples.text: ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَـٰوَٰتٍ وَمِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ مِثْلَهُنَّ يَتَنَزَّلُ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنَهُنَّ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَدْ أَحَاطَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عِلْمًۢا
examples.translation: It is Allah who has created seven heavens and of the earth, the like of them. The command descends among them, so that you may know that Allah is over all things competent and that Allah has encompassed all things in knowledge.
examples.note: Singular سَمَاء (one heaven) → many-form سَمَاوَات (heavens). Note the small inside-reshuffle: the hamza of سَمَاء becomes a و before the ـَات ending. The ـَات is still the visible many-form marker — the word is non-human, so Arabic uses the ـَات mold.

examples.text: إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلَّتِى تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِمَا يَنفَعُ ٱلنَّاسَ وَمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن مَّآءٍ فَأَحْيَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ وَتَصْرِيفِ ٱلرِّيَـٰحِ وَٱلسَّحَابِ ٱلْمُسَخَّرِ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ
examples.translation: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth, the alternation of night and day, the ships that sail in the sea... are signs for a people who use reason.
examples.note: Singular آيَة (one sign) → many-form آيَات (signs). The ة of the singular is replaced by ـَات. The opening لَـ is just an emphasis word ('truly'). Same verse also has ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ — two ـَات words in one verse, both naming many of a thing.

examples.translation: The female students are studying in the library.
examples.note: Singular طَالِبَة (one female student) → many-form الطَّالِبَات (female students). The ة of the singular gets replaced by ـَات. Same shape as مُسْلِمَات, مُؤْمِنَات in the Quranic verses — the standard many-form for groups of women.

skills.user_facing_pattern: A word with the singular intact and one of two endings tacked on: ـَانِ or ـَيْنِ. The word means exactly two of the thing.
skills.semantic_note: Arabic has a dedicated shape for exactly two of something — distinct from the singular and from the many-form. يَد (one hand) → يَدَانِ or يَدَيْنِ (two hands). وَالِد (one parent) → وَالِدَانِ or وَالِدَيْنِ (two parents — i.e., father and mother). The choice between the two endings depends on the role the word plays in the sentence.
skills.why_explanation: English has only singular and plural; Arabic has singular, dual, and plural. When a word has this dual ending, it means precisely two — not 'a couple' or 'a few,' but exactly two. The ـَانِ ending shows up when the word is the subject of a verb; the ـَيْنِ ending shows up when the word is the object, follows a preposition, or is possessed. The dual is everywhere in the Quran: ٱلْوَالِدَيْنِ (the two parents), ٱلْكَعْبَيْنِ (the two ankles), ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ (the two easts), جَنَّتَانِ (two gardens). Body parts that come in pairs almost always show up in the dual when both are meant. Because the dual is built by adding an ending without reshuffling, the singular is sitting right there inside the word — strip the ending and read what's left.

examples.translation: Lord of the two easts and Lord of the two wests.
examples.note: Singular مَشْرِق (one east, one place of sunrise) → dual ٱلْمَشْرِقَيْنِ (two easts). The ـَيْنِ ending here because the word is being possessed (it's what رَبّ 'Lord' is the lord of). Compare with ٱلْمَغْرِبَيْنِ in the same verse — the dual of مَغْرِب (one west).

examples.text: ۞ وَقَضَىٰ رَبُّكَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۚ إِمَّا يَبْلُغَنَّ عِندَكَ ٱلْكِبَرَ أَحَدُهُمَآ أَوْ كِلَاهُمَا فَلَا تَقُل لَّهُمَآ أُفٍّ وَلَا تَنْهَرْهُمَا وَقُل لَّهُمَا قَوْلًا كَرِيمًا
examples.translation: And your Lord has decreed that you worship none but Him, and to parents, good treatment. Whether one or both of them reach old age with you, do not say to them a word of contempt, and do not repel them, but speak to them a noble word.
examples.note: Singular وَالِد (one parent) → dual ٱلْوَالِدَيْنِ (the two parents — father and mother). The ـَيْنِ ending because of the بِ at the front. Strip the بِ and the ـَيْنِ ending and you get وَالِد (one parent) back. The verse also has أَحَدُهُمَا ('one of the two of them') and كِلَاهُمَا ('both of the two of them') — the surrounding pronouns also encode 'two,' not 'three or more.'

examples.translation: And for the one who fears the standing before his Lord are two gardens.
examples.note: Singular جَنَّة (one garden) → dual جَنَّتَانِ (two gardens). The ـَانِ ending because the word is the subject of the sentence ('two gardens are for the one who...'). Compare with the many-form جَنَّات (gardens, three or more) seen in 2:25 — three different shapes built from the same root: جَنَّة, جَنَّتَانِ, جَنَّات.

examples.translation: Two books are on the table.
examples.note: Singular كِتَاب (one book) → dual كِتَابَانِ (two books). The ـَانِ ending because the word is the subject of the sentence. Strip the ـَانِ and you get كِتَاب back unchanged — that's the signature of the dual.

skills.internal_label: إِنَّ wa-akhawātuhā — accusative-marking sentence-openers (إِنَّ، أَنَّ، كَأَنَّ، لَكِنَّ، لَيْتَ، لَعَلَّ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A small word at the front of a sentence — most often إِنَّ، أَنَّ، كَأَنَّ، لَكِنَّ، لَيْتَ، or لَعَلَّ — followed by a noun that takes a fatha ending. Shape: [framer word] + [noun ending in ـَ] + [the rest of the sentence]. Example: إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ — the noun right after إِنَّ ends in fatha.
skills.semantic_note: These six little words sit at the front of a sentence and color the whole statement: إِنَّ = 'truly, indeed,' أَنَّ = 'that' (introducing a clause inside another sentence, like 'I know that...'), كَأَنَّ = 'as if,' لَكِنَّ = 'but,' لَيْتَ = 'if only / would that,' لَعَلَّ = 'perhaps, hopefully.' The rule that connects all six: the noun that comes right after gets a fatha ending. So الْيَوْمُ on its own ends in damma, but إِنَّ الْيَوْمَ shifts that ending to fatha. The framer word is doing the shaping; the fatha is the visible fingerprint of that shaping.
skills.why_explanation: These six are bundled together because they all behave the same way mechanically — the noun right after them ends in fatha — even though their meanings are very different. إِنَّ in particular is everywhere in the Quran. إِنَّ اللَّهَ ... opens hundreds of statements: 'Truly, Allah is...' The fatha on اللَّهَ (you'll see it written اللَّهَ rather than اللَّهُ) is the visual signature. أَنَّ shows up inside a larger sentence to introduce a 'that' clause: 'they know that Allah...' كَأَنَّ paints a simile: 'as if they were...' لَيْتَ expresses a wish, often a regretful one — Maryam's يَا لَيْتَنِي مِتُّ ('would that I had died'). لَعَلَّ frames hope or expectation: لَعَلَّكُمْ تَتَّقُونَ ('so that you may guard yourselves'). Train your eye on the small word at the front and the fatha ending right after it — those two together tell you which framing is in play.

examples.text: يَكَادُ ٱلْبَرْقُ يَخْطَفُ أَبْصَـٰرَهُمْ ۖ كُلَّمَآ أَضَآءَ لَهُم مَّشَوْا۟ فِيهِ وَإِذَآ أَظْلَمَ عَلَيْهِمْ قَامُوا۟ ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
examples.translation: The lightning almost snatches away their sight. Every time it lights the way for them, they walk in it; and when darkness comes over them, they stand still. And if Allah had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and sight. Indeed, Allah has power over all things.
examples.note: Classic إِنَّ + اللَّهَ opening. Notice the fatha ending on اللَّهَ — on its own you'd write اللَّهُ (with a damma at the end), but the إِنَّ at the front pulls that ending to a fatha. The إِنَّ frames the sentence as a firm assertion: 'Truly, Allah has power over everything.'

examples.translation: Indeed, We have granted you al-Kawthar.
examples.note: Here إِنَّ joins with نَا ('we') and you get إِنَّا ('truly, We...'). Same framing word, but instead of a separate noun afterward, the 'we' is fused right onto it. The framing is the same: 'Truly...' opens the statement and stamps it as emphatic. The whole verse is a direct address from Allah, naming Himself as the giver.

examples.text: فَأَجَآءَهَا ٱلْمَخَاضُ إِلَىٰ جِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ قَالَتْ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَـٰذَا وَكُنتُ نَسْيًا مَّنسِيًّا
examples.translation: And the pains of childbirth drove her to the trunk of a palm tree. She said, 'Would that I had died before this and was forgotten and out of sight!'
examples.note: لَيْتَ is one of the six framers and means 'if only / would that.' Here it fuses with the يَا that calls out and the ـنِي that means 'me' — so يَا لَيْتَنِي = 'oh, would that I...' Same family as إِنَّ in how it works mechanically (a noun right after لَيْتَ would get a fatha ending), but the meaning is a wish or regret, not a firm assertion.

examples.translation: Truly, the weather is cold today.
examples.note: Plain modern usage of إِنَّ to open a statement firmly. Notice the fatha ending on الطَّقْسَ — on its own that word would end in a damma (الطَّقْسُ), but the إِنَّ in front pulls the ending to fatha. That swap is the visual signature of this whole family of framers.

skills.internal_label: كَانَ wa-akhawātuhā — defective verbs governing nominative subject + accusative predicate (كَانَ، لَيْسَ، صَارَ، أَصْبَحَ، ظَلَّ، بَاتَ، مَا زَالَ، مَا دَامَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: A small verb of being or becoming — most often كَانَ — followed by a noun (the one being described) and then a description that ends in fatha. Shape: [being-verb] + [thing] + [description ending in ـَ]. Example: كَانَ الرَّجُلُ كَرِيمًا — the description كَرِيمًا ends in fatha.
skills.semantic_note: This family of verbs links a thing to a description: كَانَ = 'was/were,' لَيْسَ = 'is/are not,' صَارَ = 'became,' أَصْبَحَ = 'became / came to be in the morning' (often just 'became'), ظَلَّ = 'kept on being,' بَاتَ = 'spent the night being,' مَا زَالَ = 'still is,' مَا دَامَ = 'as long as.' Mechanically they all behave the same: the description that comes after gets a fatha ending. So كَرِيمٌ on its own ends in damma, but كَانَ زَيْدٌ كَرِيمًا shifts the description to fatha. The being-verb is doing the linking; the fatha on the description is the visible signature of that link.
skills.why_explanation: Arabic does not always say 'is' the way English does — 'the man is generous' is often just الرَّجُلُ كَرِيمٌ with no verb at all. But once you want to say 'was,' 'became,' 'is not,' 'still is,' 'spent the night being,' you reach for this family. كَانَ is by far the most common, and it shows up everywhere in the Quran: وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا — 'And Allah is/has been forgiving, merciful.' The fatha on غَفُورًا and رَحِيمًا is the visible mark that كَانَ is doing the linking. لَيْسَ is the same family but flips the meaning to 'is not' — لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ ('there is nothing like Him'). Train your eye on these two together: the small being-verb at the front, and the fatha ending on the description that follows.

examples.text: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَخَلَقَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَبَثَّ مِنْهُمَا رِجَالًا كَثِيرًا وَنِسَآءً ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِى تَسَآءَلُونَ بِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْحَامَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ عَلَيْكُمْ رَقِيبًا
examples.translation: O mankind, fear your Lord, who created you from one soul and created from it its mate and dispersed from both of them many men and women. And fear Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs. Indeed, Allah is ever, over you, an Observer.
examples.note: كَانَ links Allah to the description رَقِيبًا ('watchful, observing'). Notice the fatha ending on رَقِيبًا — that fatha is the fingerprint of كَانَ doing the linking. كَانَ here doesn't just mean past tense; with Allah as the one described, it carries the sense of 'always has been and is.'

examples.text: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ
examples.translation: We relate to you the best of stories through what We have revealed to you of this Qur'an, and you were before it among the unaware.
examples.note: Same كَانَ family, just in the 'you' form: كُنْتَ = 'you were.' The description is the phrase 'among the unaware' — coming through مِنَ + a definite plural rather than a single fatha-ending word, but the linking job of the verb is the same: it ties the listener to a state they were in before the revelation reached them.

examples.text: وَمَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلدَّارَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ لَهِىَ ٱلْحَيَوَانُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ
examples.translation: And this worldly life is nothing but diversion and amusement. And indeed, the home of the Hereafter — that is the [true] life, if only they knew.
examples.note: كَانَ + a present-tense verb is a very common combination: كَانُوا (they were) + يَعْلَمُونَ (they know) = 'they used to know / they knew.' This is the standard way Arabic packages 'used to' or 'were doing.' Same كَانَ family member, but instead of linking to a single descriptive word it pairs with a whole verb to backshift the time.

examples.translation: The weather was cold yesterday.
examples.note: Textbook كَانَ pattern. Notice the fatha ending on بَارِدًا — on its own that word would end in damma (بَارِدٌ), but كَانَ at the front pulls the description to a fatha ending. That fatha is the visible signature of this whole family of being-verbs.

skills.internal_label: Relative pronouns الَّذِي family — definite-headed relative clauses
skills.user_facing_pattern: A word from the family الَّذِي / الَّتِي / الَّذِينَ / اللَّاتِي / اللَّائِي / اللَّذَانِ / اللَّتَانِ — sitting between a definite noun and a description, gluing them together. Shape: [the noun, definite] + [الَّذِي-form matching its gender and number] + [a verb or clause describing it]. Example: الْكِتَابُ الَّذِي قَرَأْتُهُ — 'the book that I read.'
skills.semantic_note: This family translates as 'who, which, that' in English and is used to attach a description to a definite noun. The form changes by gender and number: الَّذِي for one masculine thing, الَّتِي for one feminine thing, الَّذِينَ for many masculine, اللَّاتِي / اللَّائِي for many feminine, اللَّذَانِ / اللَّذَيْنِ for two masculine, اللَّتَانِ / اللَّتَيْنِ for two feminine. Two important habits to build: (1) Arabic uses these only when the noun being described is definite. With an indefinite noun, no relative word is used at all — رَجُلٌ قَرَأَ الْكِتَابَ ('a man who read the book') has no الَّذِي because رَجُلٌ is indefinite. (2) The verb inside the relative clause typically still names the thing it's about with a pronoun: قَرَأْتُهُ = 'I read it,' with the ـهُ pointing back to the book. So الْكِتَابُ الَّذِي قَرَأْتُهُ literally maps to 'the book — the-which — I read it.'
skills.why_explanation: الَّذِينَ is one of the most common words in the Quran. وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ — 'and give good news to those who believed.' الَّذِينَ here glues the description ('they believed') to a definite group ('those'). Same pattern with الَّذِي for one masculine thing: ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ — 'the One who created.' Watch for the matching: الَّذِينَ pairs with plural-masculine doers, الَّتِي with one feminine thing, الَّذِي with one masculine thing. The trick of Arabic relative clauses that English speakers find odd: the inside of the clause often repeats the noun with a pronoun. الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ ('who created you') — but الَّذِي رَزَقَنِي مَالًا فَأَنْفَقْتُهُ ('the one who gave me wealth, and I spent it') — that ـهُ on فَأَنْفَقْتُهُ is the pronoun pointing back to the wealth. Once you accept that pattern, these clauses unlock a huge amount of Quranic structure.

examples.text: صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
examples.translation: The path of those upon whom You have bestowed favor, not of those who have evoked anger or of those who are astray.
examples.note: الَّذِينَ is the plural-masculine form, gluing 'those' to the description 'You bestowed favor on them.' Notice the typical Arabic move: the verb أَنْعَمْتَ is followed by عَلَيْهِمْ ('on them') — the ـهِمْ pronoun points back to الَّذِينَ. So literally: 'those — whom — You bestowed favor on them.' The repetition of the reference inside the clause is normal Arabic, not a mistake.

examples.text: وَبَشِّرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ
examples.translation: And give good tidings to those who believe and do righteous deeds that they will have gardens beneath which rivers flow.
examples.note: Same الَّذِينَ form, this time gluing 'those' to two verbs: 'they believed' and 'they did righteous deeds.' The الَّذِينَ + verb pattern is the most common Quranic frame for naming the believers. Note how the verbs ءَامَنُوا and عَمِلُوا are plural masculine, matching الَّذِينَ — Arabic insists on this matching.

examples.text: هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنْهُ ءَايَـٰتٌ مُّحْكَمَـٰتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ
examples.translation: It is He who sent down to you the Book in which are precise verses — they are the foundation of the Book.
examples.note: Singular-masculine form الَّذِي, gluing هُوَ ('He') to the description أَنزَلَ ('sent down'). The whole clause names Allah as the one who revealed. This is the most common shape of all: pronoun + الَّذِي + verb — 'He who [did X].' Train your eye on it; you'll meet it in dozens of verses.

examples.text: هَذَا هُوَ الكِتَابُ الَّذِي اشْتَرَيْتُهُ أَمْسِ.
examples.translation: This is the book that I bought yesterday.
examples.note: Single masculine thing (الكِتَاب) so the form is الَّذِي. Inside the clause you see the typical move: اشْتَرَيْتُ = 'I bought' and then ـهُ = 'it,' with the ـهُ pointing back to the book. Literally: 'the book — the-which — I bought it.' Compare this to the English 'the book that I bought,' where English drops the 'it' — Arabic keeps it.

skills.internal_label: لَا + indicative imperfect — general/habitual present negation
skills.user_facing_pattern: The little word لَا sitting in front of a present-tense verb. Shape: لَا + [present-tense verb, ending unchanged]. Example: لَا يَفْعَلُ — 'he does not do.' The verb keeps its normal damma ending after لَا.
skills.semantic_note: لَa in front of a present-tense verb negates the action in a general or ongoing way: لَا يَعْلَمُ = 'he does not know,' لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ = 'they do not believe.' The key feature compared to other negators: the verb's ending stays the way it normally is in the present tense (damma at the end for sound singular forms: يَعْلَمُ → لَا يَعْلَمُ — the damma stays). Contrast with لَمْ and لَنْ, which change the ending. لَا is the everyday, unmarked way to say 'does not / do not' for ongoing or habitual situations.
skills.why_explanation: This is one of the simplest and most common patterns in the Quran. لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ ('they do not believe') closes 2:6. لَا تَأْخُذُهُ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ from Ayat al-Kursi — 'no drowsiness overtakes Him, nor sleep.' The pattern is dead simple: see لَا right before a present-tense verb, read it as 'does not / do not / will not (in general).' Don't confuse this لَا with the لَا that sits in front of nouns to mean 'no' (لَا إِلَهَ — 'no god'). Same word, different jobs: in front of a verb it negates the verb; in front of a noun it negates the noun's existence. The eye-test is whether what comes after is a verb shape or a noun shape.

examples.text: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ
examples.translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve — it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them — they will not believe.
examples.note: Textbook لَا + present-tense verb. The verb يُؤْمِنُونَ keeps its full plural ending — لَا doesn't change the verb's shape, it just negates it. The reading is general: 'they do not believe' as an ongoing state.

examples.text: وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلَا تُشْرِكُوا۟ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَبِٱلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ إِحْسَـٰنًا ۖ ... ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ مَن كَانَ مُخْتَالًا فَخُورًا
examples.translation: Worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him. And to parents, do good. ... Indeed, Allah does not love those who are arrogant and boastful.
examples.note: Same pattern, present-tense verb يُحِبُّ ('loves') with لَا in front. The damma at the end of يُحِبُّ stays — لَا doesn't disturb the ending. The reading is general/timeless: 'does not love' as a standing truth, not a one-time event.

examples.text: لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا
examples.translation: Allah does not burden a soul beyond its capacity.
examples.note: Same simple shape: لَا + present-tense verb, with the verb keeping its full ending. The reading is timeless and general: 'does not burden' as an ongoing principle.

examples.translation: I do not drink coffee.
examples.note: Plain everyday negation. لَا + present-tense verb أَشْرَبُ ('I drink'). The damma at the end of أَشْرَبُ stays untouched. Reading: a general habit — 'I don't drink coffee' as a standing fact about the speaker.

skills.internal_label: لَمْ + jussive imperfect — past negation with mood-shifted verb form
skills.user_facing_pattern: The little word لَمْ in front of a verb that looks like a present-tense verb but with a sukun on the final letter instead of a damma. Shape: لَمْ + [present-tense verb shape, ending in sukun]. Example: لَمْ يَفْعَلْ — 'he did not do.' The verb يَفْعَلُ would normally end in damma; after لَمْ it ends in sukun.
skills.semantic_note: Two things to notice. First, لَمْ negates the past — even though the verb after it looks like a present-tense shape (يَفْعَلْ, not فَعَلَ), the meaning is past: 'he did not do.' This is unique to Arabic: the present-tense shape of the verb gets reused to express a negated past. Second, the ending of the verb shifts. يَفْعَلُ on its own ends in damma; after لَمْ that damma drops to a sukun (silent ending), so you read يَفْعَلْ. For verbs ending in vowels the change is different (the vowel often drops entirely), but the principle is the same: لَمْ shortens the verb's ending.
skills.why_explanation: This is one of the trickier patterns for beginners because it looks present but means past. Surat al-Ikhlas closes with لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ — 'He did not beget, nor was He begotten.' Both verbs (يَلِدْ and يُولَدْ) look like present-tense shapes, and both end in sukun rather than damma. The لَمْ at the front is what flips both readings to past and pulls the endings to sukun. The other major past negator is مَا followed by a regular past-tense verb (مَا فَعَلَ). The two overlap in meaning but لَمْ is more compact and very common in formal/Quranic Arabic. Train your eye: see لَمْ + a present-shaped verb with a sukun ending, read it as 'did not [do].'

examples.translation: He neither begets nor is born.
examples.note: Two لَمْ + present-shaped verbs back to back. يَلِدْ on its own would be يَلِدُ (with damma); after لَمْ the damma drops to a sukun and the meaning shifts to past: 'did not beget.' Same with يُولَدْ ('was not born'). The English translation here uses present tense to convey timelessness, but the verb mechanics are firmly the لَمْ + sukun-ending shape.

examples.text: إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَأَنذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ
examples.translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve — it is all the same for them whether you warn them or do not warn them — they will not believe.
examples.note: Same verse where you saw لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ. Now look at لَمْ تُنذِرْهُمْ. The verb تُنذِرُ ('you warn') would normally end in damma; after لَمْ the damma drops and the meaning becomes past: 'you did not warn them.' Side-by-side with لَا on the same verse — لَا keeps the damma and reads as general/ongoing, لَمْ shortens the ending and reads as past.

examples.translation: Does he not know that Allah sees?
examples.note: أَ at the front is the question marker, then لَمْ + the verb يَعْلَمْ. The verb يَعْلَمُ would normally end in damma; after لَمْ that damma drops to sukun, and the meaning is the past-flavored 'did not know / does not know.' The question framing — 'does he not know?' — is a rhetorical rebuke: of course he should know.

examples.translation: I did not go to the school yesterday.
examples.note: Plain modern usage. أَذْهَبُ on its own ('I go') ends in damma. After لَمْ the damma drops to sukun, giving أَذْهَبْ, and the meaning shifts to past: 'I did not go.' The presence of أَمْسِ ('yesterday') makes the past reading explicit, but the لَمْ + shortened ending already carries it.

skills.internal_label: لَنْ + subjunctive imperfect — emphatic future negation
skills.user_facing_pattern: The little word لَنْ in front of a verb that looks like a present-tense verb but with a fatha on the final letter instead of a damma. Shape: لَنْ + [present-tense verb shape, ending in fatha]. Example: لَنْ يَفْعَلَ — 'he will not do (and won't).' The verb يَفْعَلُ would normally end in damma; after لَنْ it ends in fatha.
skills.semantic_note: لَنْ negates the future, often with a sense of firmness — 'will not, and won't.' Two things to notice: the time reading shifts to future (even though the verb shape itself is the present-tense form), and the ending of the verb shifts from damma to fatha. So يَفْعَلُ → لَنْ يَفْعَلَ. Compare the three negators side by side: لَا يَفْعَلُ (general 'does not'), لَمْ يَفْعَلْ (past 'did not'), لَنْ يَفْعَلَ (future 'will not'). The verb shape is the same in all three cases; only the negator and the final vowel change, and those two changes together tell you the time.
skills.why_explanation: Each of the three negators (لَا، لَمْ، لَنْ) does its own job and leaves its own signature on the verb's ending. لَنْ is the future one and it pulls the verb's ending to fatha. In 2:80 the disbelievers say لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ ('the Fire will never touch us') — تَمَسَّنَا has its ending pulled to fatha by the لَنْ in front. Many translators render لَنْ as 'will never' to capture its emphatic future flavor, though plain 'will not' is also fine. The pattern is mechanical: لَنْ + present-shaped verb + fatha ending = 'will not [do].'

examples.text: وَقَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً ۚ قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ
examples.translation: And they said, 'Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few days.' Say, 'Have you taken a covenant with Allah? For Allah will never break His covenant.'
examples.note: Two لَنْ + future-negation pairs in the same verse. تَمَسَّنَا ('it touches us') gets its ending pulled to fatha by the لَنْ in front. Same pattern again with يُخْلِفَ ('breaks') — the damma you'd see on it normally is now a fatha after لَنْ. The verse uses لَنْ to render firm refusals: 'never will...' on both sides.

examples.text: وَقَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً ۚ قُلْ أَتَّخَذْتُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ عَهْدًا فَلَن يُخْلِفَ ٱللَّهُ عَهْدَهُۥٓ
examples.translation: And they said, 'Never will the Fire touch us, except for a few days.' Say, 'Have you taken a covenant with Allah? For Allah will never break His covenant.'
examples.note: Highlighting the second لَنْ on its own. يُخْلِفَ ends in fatha here — pulled there by the لَنْ. The Quranic context attributes the action (or non-action) to Allah Himself: He will never break His covenant. The لَنْ + fatha ending is the visible signature of the future-negation framing.

examples.translation: What put you into the Fire of Hell?
examples.note: Quick comparison verse: this uses مَا + a past-tense verb to negate (or here, to ask 'what...'). Set it next to لَنْ + present-shaped verb with fatha ending and you can see the contrast. لَنْ targets the future and reshapes the verb's ending; مَا often just sits in front of an unchanged past-tense verb. (Note: this verse uses مَا as 'what,' but the pattern of مَا + past-tense verb is the same shape used for past negation.)

examples.translation: I will never forget this day.
examples.note: Standard emphatic future negation. أَنْسَى is the 'I forget' shape; with لَنْ in front the meaning becomes 'I will never forget.' For verbs ending in a long vowel like أَنْسَى the visible ending change is subtle (it's a different ending category), but the لَنْ in front still does its job: future negation, with a firm flavor.

skills.internal_label: لَيْسَ — defective verb negating nominal sentences (acts like كَانَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: The word لَيْسَ at the front of a sentence, followed by a noun, then a description that ends in fatha — or sometimes the description is bundled with بِـ + a kasra ending instead. Shape A: لَيْسَ + [thing] + [description ending in ـَ]. Shape B: لَيْسَ + [thing] + بِـ + [description ending in kasra]. Example A: لَيْسَ زَيْدٌ كَرِيمًا. Example B: لَيْسَ زَيْدٌ بِكَرِيمٍ. Both mean 'Zayd is not generous.'
skills.semantic_note: لَيْسَ means 'is not / are not' and works as the negative pair of كَانَ — same family, same mechanics. The thing being described stays in its normal shape; the description after it gets a fatha ending. There's also a second common shape: instead of a plain fatha-ending description, you can stick a بِـ in front of the description, and then the description takes a kasra ending. The بِـ adds a touch of emphasis. Both shapes mean the same thing. لَيْسَ is also slightly unusual — it always means 'is not' (present), even though it looks like a past-tense verb shape.
skills.why_explanation: Arabic doesn't usually need a verb to say 'is' (الرَّجُلُ كَرِيمٌ — 'the man is generous,' no verb). But to say 'is not,' you need لَيْسَ. The famous opening of Surat al-Ikhlas, لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ, uses لَمْ for past negation; if the verse wanted to say 'He is not begotten' as a present statement, لَيْسَ would be the tool. In 3:182, وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ — 'and that Allah is not at all unjust to the servants' — لَيْسَ + بِـ + the description in kasra (بِظَلَّامٍ). The بِـ here adds force: 'in no way unjust.' Train your eye on both shapes: plain fatha-ending description, or بِـ + kasra-ending description. Both follow لَيْسَ, both negate.

examples.text: ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ
examples.translation: That is for what your hands have put forth, and because Allah is not ever unjust to His servants.
examples.note: Classic لَيْسَ + بِـ + description-in-kasra shape. ظَلَّامٍ ends in kasra because of the بِـ in front. The بِـ adds emphasis to the negation: 'not at all unjust, in no way unjust.' Without the بِـ you would see لَيْسَ ظَلَّامًا (with the description ending in fatha) and the meaning would be the same but slightly less forceful.

examples.text: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ
examples.translation: And of the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' but they are not believers.
examples.note: Same negation effect using a different tool: مَا + بِـ + description-in-kasra. مَا here functions just like لَيْسَ — it negates the 'they are' sense, and the بِـ + kasra ending on مُؤْمِنِينَ matches the لَيْسَ بِـ shape. So you can read this as a relative of the لَيْسَ pattern: same job (negate a 'they are'), slightly different tool.

examples.text: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ
examples.translation: Allah — there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep.
examples.note: Comparison example. This verse doesn't use لَيْسَ; it uses لَا + a present-tense verb to negate. Worth contrasting: لَيْسَ negates a state ('is not [a thing/quality]'), while لَا + verb negates an action ('does not [do]'). Side by side they help you feel what each tool is for.

examples.translation: The weather is not cold today.
examples.note: Plain لَيْسَ shape. The thing (الجَوُّ) keeps its normal damma ending; the description بَارِدًا ends in fatha — the visible signature of لَيْسَ doing its linking-and-negating job. Compare this to كَانَ الجَوُّ بَارِدًا ('the weather was cold') — same mechanics, opposite meaning.

skills.internal_label: مَا — versatile negator for past, present, and nominal sentences
skills.user_facing_pattern: The little word مَا sitting in front of a verb (past or present) or a noun phrase. Shape A: مَا + [past-tense verb] = 'did not [do].' Shape B: مَا + [present-tense verb] = 'does not / is not [doing].' Shape C: مَا + [pronoun/noun] + بِـ + [description] = 'X is not...' Examples: مَا فَعَلَ ('he did not do'), مَا يَعْلَمُ ('he does not know'), مَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ ('they are not believers').
skills.semantic_note: مَا is the most flexible of the negators. Unlike لَمْ (past only) or لَنْ (future only), مَا can sit in front of a past-tense verb, a present-tense verb, or even a noun phrase. مَا فَعَلَ and لَمْ يَفْعَلْ both mean 'he did not do,' but مَا is older / more emphatic in flavor in some contexts and is heavily used in the Quran. Two important quirks: (1) when مَا negates a 'they are' kind of sentence, you often see بِـ added to the description, exactly like with لَيْسَ — مَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ. (2) مَا has another life as a relative pronoun ('what, that which') and as a question word ('what?'). Context tells you which is which. As a negator it sits in front of a verb or a small sentence; as 'what' it sits at the front of a question.
skills.why_explanation: مَا is everywhere in the Quran as a negator. مَا وَدَّعَكَ رَبُّكَ وَمَا قَلَىٰ — 'Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor has He been displeased' (93:3). Two مَا + past-tense verbs back to back, both negating past actions. Compare with لَمْ يَلِدْ وَلَمْ يُولَدْ — same kind of negation but using لَمْ + present-shaped verb instead of مَا + past-tense verb. Both routes work; they just have different flavors and slightly different rhythms. مَا أَدْرِي ('I do not know') and مَا يَعْلَمُ ('he does not know') are also extremely common. Train your eye: مَا at the front of a verb-led sentence is almost always a negator. مَا at the front of a question is 'what?' مَا right after a verb is often the relative pronoun 'what / that which.' Position is the key.

examples.translation: Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor has He been displeased.
examples.note: Two مَا + past-tense verbs. وَدَّعَ = 'he forsook' and قَلَى = 'he was displeased,' both in their plain past shape; مَا at the front negates each one. This is the canonical 'past negation' use of مَا — equivalent in meaning to لَمْ يُوَدِّعْكَ ... وَلَمْ يَقْلِ but using a different tool. The verse comforts the Prophet: Allah has not done either of those things.

examples.text: وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ
examples.translation: And no one knows its [true] interpretation except Allah. But those firm in knowledge say, 'We believe in it.'
examples.note: مَا + present-tense verb, paired with إِلَّا ('except') to make 'no one knows... except Allah.' This is one of the most common Quranic uses of مَا: مَا + verb + إِلَّا + [name] = 'none [does] except [name].' Here the verb يَعْلَمُ keeps its full damma ending (مَا doesn't reshape it the way لَمْ would).

examples.text: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَقُولُ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَمَا هُم بِمُؤْمِنِينَ
examples.translation: And of the people are some who say, 'We believe in Allah and the Last Day,' but they are not believers.
examples.note: مَا used to negate a 'they are' sentence — exactly the same job لَيْسَ does. Notice the بِـ added to the description مُؤْمِنِينَ, just as with لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ. So مَا has three jobs in one: negate past verbs, negate present verbs, and negate 'is/are' sentences. The same little word, used in three patterns.

examples.translation: I have not read the book.
examples.note: Plain مَا + past-tense verb. قَرَأْتُ ('I read,' past) keeps its full shape; مَا at the front negates it. Equivalent in meaning to لَمْ أَقْرَأِ الكِتَابَ, just using a different tool. Both are correct; مَا feels slightly older or more literary, while لَمْ is the everyday choice in modern Standard Arabic.

skills.internal_label: particle إِنْ — real/possible conditional, governs jussive in both protasis and apodosis
skills.user_facing_pattern: إِنْ at the start of a sentence sets up an 'if' clause for something that may or may not happen. The verbs in both halves drop their final short vowel — you'll see a sukun (or no vowel mark) where you'd expect a ـُ.
skills.semantic_note: إِنْ presents the condition as genuinely uncertain — could happen, could not. Compare with إِذَا, which treats the condition as something that's expected to happen or already happens. إِنْ تَنْصُرُوا اللَّهَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ = 'if you help Allah, He will help you' — both تَنْصُرُوا and يَنْصُر have lost their final vowel.
skills.why_explanation: The signature trick of إِنْ sentences is the matching pair of verbs that have both shed their final short vowel. Once you train your eye to spot that double sukun pattern, the 'if-then' meaning falls into place even before you parse the rest. This is one of the cleanest grammar signals in Quranic Arabic and worth memorizing as a visual unit.

examples.text: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تَنصُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَنصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَكُمْ
examples.translation: O you who believe, if you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet firm.
examples.note: Three verbs in the chain — تَنصُرُوا, يَنصُرْ, يُثَبِّتْ — all stripped of the vowel they'd normally carry. That's the إِنْ pattern doing its work.

examples.text: وَإِن كُنتُمْ فِى رَيْبٍ مِّمَّا نَزَّلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا فَأْتُوا۟ بِسُورَةٍ مِّن مِّثْلِهِۦ
examples.translation: And if you are in doubt about what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a chapter like it.
examples.note: Here the 'then' half starts with فَـ followed by a command — a common variant when the result is an instruction rather than a verb in the present.

examples.text: وَإِن كَانَ كَبُرَ عَلَيْكَ إِعْرَاضُهُمْ فَإِنِ ٱسْتَطَعْتَ أَن تَبْتَغِىَ نَفَقًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ
examples.translation: And if their turning away has become hard on you, then if you are able to seek out a tunnel in the earth...
examples.note: Two إِنْ clauses stacked — the second nested inside the first. The kasra under إِنِ is just the connecting sound when the next word starts with ا.

examples.translation: If you study, you'll succeed.
examples.note: A textbook two-verb إِنْ sentence — both verbs end in sukun. Modern Arabic often prefers إِذَا here, but the إِنْ form survives in proverbs and formal writing.

skills.internal_label: particle إِذَا — temporal/realized conditional, typically followed by perfective verb
skills.user_facing_pattern: إِذَا at the start of a clause means 'when' or 'if' for something that's expected to happen or actually does happen. The verb right after إِذَا is usually in the past form, even when the meaning is future or general.
skills.semantic_note: إِذَا doesn't really doubt the condition — it treats it as a realistic scenario about to unfold. إِذَا جَاءَ نَصْرُ اللَّهِ = 'when the help of Allah comes' — the verb جَاءَ looks past, but the meaning here is forward-looking. Contrast with إِنْ, which keeps the condition genuinely open.
skills.why_explanation: The mismatch between past-shaped verb and future meaning trips up most learners. Read it as a snapshot: إِذَا paints the scene as if it's already happening, and the past form is part of that 'it's as good as done' feel. Quranic eschatology leans heavily on this — verses about the Day all use إِذَا plus a past-form verb to make the event feel inevitable rather than hypothetical.

examples.translation: When the help of Allah and the victory come.
examples.note: جَاءَ is past-shaped but the verse describes a future arrival. إِذَا is what does that work — it sets the scene as a coming reality.

examples.translation: When the sun is folded up.
examples.note: Surah 81 opens with a long chain of إِذَا scenes — each one a snapshot of the Last Day, all using past-form verbs for that 'as if already happening' feel.

examples.translation: When the earth is shaken with its shaking.
examples.note: Same pattern — إِذَا plus past-form verb describing a future cosmic event.

examples.translation: When you arrive, call me.
examples.note: وَصَلْتَ is past-shaped, but the meaning is 'once you've arrived' — future from the speaker's point of view. This is the standard إِذَا pattern in everyday Arabic.

skills.internal_label: particle لَوْ — counterfactual conditional, frequently with لَـ-prefixed apodosis
skills.user_facing_pattern: لَوْ sets up an 'if it had been so, but it wasn't' clause. The result half very often starts with لَـ attached to a past-form verb. So the shape is: لَوْ ... لَـ + past verb.
skills.semantic_note: لَوْ signals a hypothetical that's contrary to fact. لَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً = 'had Allah willed, He would have made you one community.' The verse implies: He didn't will that, so it didn't happen. Compare with إِنْ (genuinely uncertain) and إِذَا (expected to happen) — لَوْ is the one that says 'this didn't happen.'
skills.why_explanation: The لَوْ ... لَـ pairing is a tight visual signature in the Quran. Once you spot لَوْ at the head of a clause and a لَـ glued to the next main verb, you know you're reading a counterfactual — 'had it gone this way, then this would have followed.' The Quran uses this pattern often when describing what Allah could have done but chose not to, leaving moral and theological space for the present situation.

examples.text: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ
examples.translation: Had Allah willed, He would have made you one single community — but He leaves astray whom He wills and guides whom He wills.
examples.note: Classic لَوْ ... لَـ pair. The verse goes on to say what actually is — a counterfactual that grounds the real situation.

examples.text: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَذَهَبَ بِسَمْعِهِمْ وَأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ
examples.translation: Had Allah willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight.
examples.note: Same shape: لَوْ شَاءَ followed by لَـ on the result verb. The didn't-happen reading is built into the grammar.

examples.text: وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَمَعَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ
examples.translation: Had Allah willed, He would have gathered them on guidance.
examples.note: A third instance of the same construction — لَوْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ paired with لَـ + past verb is one of the Quran's most repeated grammatical units.

examples.translation: Had I known, I would have told you.
examples.note: Same لَوْ ... لَـ shape in modern usage. The implication: I didn't know, and I didn't tell you.

skills.internal_label: definite article الـ — assimilation with sun letters vs full pronunciation with moon letters
skills.user_facing_pattern: الـ stuck to the front of a noun makes it specific — 'the X' rather than 'a X'. With about half the alphabet (sun letters), the ل of الـ gets swallowed and you double the next letter instead: الشَّمْس, الرَّحْمَن. With the other half (moon letters), the ل is pronounced clearly: القَمَر, الكِتَاب.
skills.semantic_note: الكِتَاب = 'the book' (a specific known book). كِتَاب = 'a book.' Quranic phrases like ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ rest on this distinction — 'the praise' (all of it) belongs to Allah. Sun letters are the ones whose tongue position pulls the ل into them: t, th, d, dh, r, z, s, sh, ṣ, ḍ, ṭ, ẓ, l, n. Moon letters are the rest.
skills.why_explanation: The trick is that الـ always looks the same in writing — what changes is the sound. When you see ٱلشَّمْس, the ل is silent and the ش is doubled by the shadda. When you see ٱلْقَمَر, the ل carries a sukun and gets pronounced. Reading aloud trains this faster than memorizing the sun-letter list. After a few weeks of recitation you stop thinking about it.

examples.translation: All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all worlds.
examples.note: ٱلْحَمْد and ٱلْعَالَمِين both have moon letters (ح, ع) right after الـ — the ل sound is heard clearly in both.

examples.translation: In the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate.
examples.note: Both words start with ر, a sun letter — the ل of الـ is silent and the ر is doubled. You hear 'ar-Rahman, ar-Rahim,' not 'al-Rahman, al-Rahim.'

examples.translation: That is the Book — no doubt is in it.
examples.note: ك is a moon letter, so الْكِتَاب reads 'al-kitab' with the ل clearly heard. The الـ marks this as the specific Book under discussion.

examples.translation: The student is at home.
examples.note: ط is a sun letter (الطَّالِب = 'aṭ-ṭālib'), ب is a moon letter (البَيْت = 'al-bayt'). Same الـ in writing, two different sounds.

skills.internal_label: tanwin — three case-marked indefinite endings ـً ـٌ ـٍ adding a final /n/ sound
skills.user_facing_pattern: Three little marks at the end of a word — ـً (double fatha), ـٌ (double damma), ـٍ (double kasra) — each add a quick 'n' sound after the regular short vowel. كِتَابٌ = 'kitabun,' a book. The mark used depends on the word's role in the sentence.
skills.semantic_note: Tanwin signals 'a, some, any' — the opposite of الـ. كِتَابٌ = 'a book.' الكِتَابُ = 'the book.' Which of the three flavors you see (ـً, ـٌ, ـٍ) tracks the noun's job: subject-ish positions tend to get ـٌ, direct-object-ish positions ـً, and 'after a preposition' positions ـٍ. The 'n' sound is the giveaway that the noun is indefinite.
skills.why_explanation: The simplest entry point is to listen for the 'n.' Anywhere you hear a short vowel followed by 'n' at the end of a noun in recitation, you're hearing tanwin, and the meaning is 'a/an' or 'some.' The three flavors carry extra information about role in the sentence, but for reading comprehension, just registering 'this noun is indefinite' goes a long way. The role distinctions become more useful later when you start parsing complex sentences.

examples.translation: And for them is a great punishment.
examples.note: Both nouns end in ـٌ — 'adhabun adhīmun.' Indefinite, and in the subject-ish position. Notice how the descriptive word 'great' takes the same ـٌ ending to match.

examples.translation: Indeed Satan is a clear enemy to mankind.
examples.note: Same pattern — 'aduwwun mubīnun,' two ـٌ endings on a noun and its descriptor.

examples.translation: Indeed We have given you al-Kawthar.
examples.note: A counter-example for contrast: ٱلْكَوْثَر has الـ, not tanwin — so it's 'the Kawthar' (the specific named thing), not 'a kawthar.'

examples.translation: I bought a new book.
examples.note: Both words end in ـً — 'kitāban jadīdan.' Indefinite, and in the direct-object position after a verb.

skills.internal_label: vocative particle يَا — direct address marker, often with أَيُّهَا for definite-noun address
skills.user_facing_pattern: يَا at the start of a phrase means 'O ___' — you're calling or addressing whoever comes next. يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ = 'O people.' يَا قَوْمِ = 'O my people.' يَا بُنَيَّ = 'O my dear son.'
skills.semantic_note: يَا is the basic 'hey, you' marker. When the person being addressed has الـ in front of them, Arabic inserts أَيُّهَا (for masculine) or أَيَّتُهَا (for feminine) between يَا and the noun: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ, not يَا النَّاسُ. When the person is being addressed with 'my X,' the ـِي ending often shrinks to just a kasra: يَا قَوْمِ instead of يَا قَوْمِي.
skills.why_explanation: Quranic exhortations almost all open with this particle. يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا, يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ, يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ — each is a hinge that says 'now I'm speaking directly to this group.' Spotting يَا instantly tells you a direct command or call is coming. The shrunken ـِي in يَا قَوْمِ is one of those small surface oddities worth memorizing once so it stops looking strange.

examples.text: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمُ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُمْ
examples.translation: O people, worship your Lord who created you.
examples.note: Because النَّاس has الـ, you can't say يَا النَّاس directly — أَيُّهَا is the bridge. This is the most repeated direct-address opener in the Quran.

examples.text: قَالَ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تَقْصُصْ رُءْيَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ إِخْوَتِكَ
examples.translation: He said, 'O my dear son, do not tell your dream to your brothers.'
examples.note: بُنَيَّ is a tender form of 'my son.' The ـيَّ at the end is the 'my' suffix combined with this address shape.

examples.text: قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥ
examples.translation: He said, 'O my people, worship Allah — you have no god other than Him.'
examples.note: يَا قَوْمِ — 'O my people,' with the 'my' shrunk down to just a kasra under the م. This is the standard prophetic opening across many surahs.

examples.translation: Ahmad, come here.
examples.note: Simple direct address with a name — no أَيُّهَا needed because أَحْمَد doesn't have الـ.

skills.internal_label: exception particle إِلَّا — typically following negation to form an exclusive 'no X except Y' frame
skills.user_facing_pattern: إِلَّا means 'except.' The standard frame is 'no/not X ... إِلَّا Y' — 'no X except Y.' لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ = 'no god except Allah.' The negation comes first, then إِلَّا cuts the one exception out of the wider denial.
skills.semantic_note: إِلَّا paired with negation produces an exclusive statement, not a partial one. لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ doesn't say 'there are several gods, one of whom is Allah' — it says 'no god at all, except Allah.' The structure first denies broadly, then carves out a single survivor. مَا رَأَيْتُ إِلَّا أَحْمَدَ = 'I saw no one except Ahmad' = 'I only saw Ahmad.'
skills.why_explanation: This is one of Arabic's tightest logical structures and the backbone of the shahada. The two-step rhythm — negate everything, then admit exactly one thing — gives the construction its emphatic feel. In Quranic argumentation, إِلَّا often introduces the one true alternative after a sweeping denial, and the negation that comes before it is doing as much work as the exception itself.

examples.text: فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ
examples.translation: So know that there is no god except Allah.
examples.note: The textbook example. لَا sweeps the field clean ('no god'), then إِلَّا carves out the single exception.

examples.text: ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ
examples.translation: Allah — there is no god except He, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer.
examples.note: Same frame, with the 'one exception' being the pronoun هُوَ ('He') instead of the name.

examples.translation: And no one knows its interpretation except Allah.
examples.note: The negation here is مَا instead of لَا, but the إِلَّا frame works identically — sweeping denial, then one exception.

examples.translation: I drink nothing but water.
examples.note: Same structure with a verb: deny broadly ('I drink no'), then admit one thing ('except water').

skills.internal_label: demonstratives — هَذَا/هَذِهِ/هَؤُلَاءِ (proximal) vs ذَلِكَ/تِلْكَ/أُولَئِكَ (distal), gender and number agreement
skills.user_facing_pattern: Words for 'this/that.' For things near you: هَذَا (one masculine thing), هَذِهِ (one feminine thing), هَؤُلَاءِ (more than one, any gender). For things farther away or already mentioned: ذَلِكَ (one masculine), تِلْكَ (one feminine), أُولَئِكَ (more than one).
skills.semantic_note: هَذَا الكِتَابُ = 'this book' (right here). ذَلِكَ الكِتَابُ = 'that book' (over there, or the one previously mentioned). When a demonstrative is paired with a noun, the noun nearly always carries الـ. The 'far' set (ذَلِكَ, تِلْكَ, أُولَئِكَ) often shows up in the Quran pointing back at something just established or pointing to something elevated and distant in stature.
skills.why_explanation: The distance distinction in Arabic isn't just physical. ذَلِكَ الكِتَابُ at the start of Surah al-Baqara is famously discussed by commentators precisely because 'that' feels strange for a book in your hands — many read it as a marker of the Book's elevated status, not its physical location. So when you see the 'far' set, ask whether the distance is spatial, temporal, or honorific. The plural forms (هَؤُلَاءِ, أُولَئِكَ) collapse the gender distinction, which makes them easier to recognize.

examples.text: ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ لَا رَيْبَ ۛ فِيهِ ۛ هُدًى لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ
examples.translation: That is the Book — no doubt is in it — guidance for those mindful of Allah.
examples.note: ذَٰلِكَ paired with ٱلْكِتَاب — 'that Book.' Note الـ on الكِتَاب: demonstratives almost always pair with a definite noun.

examples.translation: Have you seen the one who denies the recompense?
examples.note: ٱلَّذِي is a close cousin of the demonstratives — 'the one who.' It works like 'that' but introduces a relative clause about who the person is.

examples.translation: So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?
examples.note: أَيّ is a close relative — 'which.' It picks one item out of a group, like 'this' picks out one specifically.

examples.text: هَذِهِ السَّيَّارَةُ سَرِيعَةٌ، أَمَّا تِلْكَ فَبَطِيئَةٌ
examples.translation: This car is fast — that one, on the other hand, is slow.
examples.note: Both feminine forms — هَذِهِ for the near car, تِلْكَ for the far one. سَيَّارَة is feminine, so the demonstratives match.

skills.internal_label: attached pronominal suffixes — possessive on nouns, object on verbs, identical forms except 1st-singular ـِي vs ـنِي
skills.user_facing_pattern: Short endings glued to the back of a word that mean 'my, your, his, her, our, their' on nouns, or 'me, you, him, her, us, them' on verbs. Same shapes either way, with one exception: 'me' is ـنِي but 'my' is ـِي. Common ones: ـِي/ـنِي (my/me), ـكَ (your, to a male), ـكِ (your, to a female), ـهُ (his/him), ـهَا (her), ـنَا (our/us), ـكُمْ (your, to a group), ـهُمْ (their/them).
skills.semantic_note: كِتَابُهُ = 'his book.' رَأَيْتُهُ = 'I saw him.' Same ـهُ, different jobs depending on whether it sits on a noun or a verb. The only suffix that changes shape between the two roles is the first-person singular: ـِي for 'my' (كِتَابِي = 'my book') but ـنِي for 'me' (رَأَيْتَنِي = 'you saw me'). The extra ن in ـنِي keeps 'me' from getting confused with verb endings.
skills.why_explanation: These suffixes are the densest meaning-per-letter machinery in Arabic. A two-letter ending can carry person, number, gender, and grammatical role at once. Quranic phrases like بِسْمِهِ, لَهُ, بِهِ, إِنَّهُ all end in attached pronouns, and reading fluency depends on instant recognition. Train them by collecting short pairs: كِتَابِي / كِتَابُكَ / كِتَابُهُ / كِتَابُنَا — same noun, swapping endings, lets you hear how each ending lands.

examples.translation: In the name of Allah, the Most Merciful, the Most Compassionate.
examples.note: The most famous attached-pronoun phrase comes a verse later — but here بِسْم itself is just 'in the name (of).' The next verses repeatedly use ـهُ for 'his' and ـكَ for 'your.'

examples.translation: Your Lord has not forsaken you, nor has He become displeased.
examples.note: Two ـكَ in one short verse — وَدَّعَكَ ('he forsook you,' object on a verb) and رَبُّكَ ('your Lord,' possessive on a noun). Same letter, two roles.

examples.text: وَنَادَىٰ نُوحٌ ٱبْنَهُۥ وَكَانَ فِى مَعْزِلٍ
examples.translation: And Nuh called his son, who was apart from them.
examples.note: ٱبْنَهُ = 'his son' — ـهُ glued to the end carrying 'his.' The little و-shape after ه is just how scribes mark the long u sound.

examples.translation: Our house is near your house.
examples.note: ـنَا = 'our,' ـكُمْ = 'your (plural).' Same noun بَيْت with different endings.

skills.internal_label: interrogative words — content questions (مَا/مَنْ/مَتَى/أَيْنَ/كَيْفَ/كَمْ/لِمَاذَا) and yes-no markers (هَلْ/أَ)
skills.user_facing_pattern: Question words that sit at the start of a sentence: مَا or مَاذَا (what), مَنْ (who), مَتَى (when), أَيْنَ (where), كَيْفَ (how), كَمْ (how many), لِمَاذَا or لِمَ (why). For yes-no questions, Arabic uses هَلْ at the start, or just an أ glued to the first word.
skills.semantic_note: مَنْ خَلَقَكَ؟ = 'who created you?' كَيْفَ حَالُكَ؟ = 'how are you?' هَلْ تَعْرِفُهُ؟ = 'do you know him?' For yes-no, أَتَعْرِفُهُ has the same meaning as هَلْ تَعْرِفُهُ — the أ is a question marker fused onto the front. Watch out for مَا, which can also mean 'not' (negation) — context tells you which one.
skills.why_explanation: The Quran uses interrogatives constantly, often rhetorically. أَفَلَا يَنْظُرُونَ — the أ at the front turns the whole 'do they not look' into a question, with فَلَا inside meaning 'not.' Recognizing question markers fast keeps you from misreading rhetorical questions as plain statements. The dual meaning of مَا (what / not) is the most common stumbling block — practice pairs like مَا هَذَا ('what is this') against مَا أَكَلْتُ ('I did not eat') until the difference clicks from context.

examples.translation: And what will make you know what the Striking Calamity is?
examples.note: Two مَا in one verse, both meaning 'what.' Quranic style often stacks interrogatives like this for emphasis.

examples.text: أَفَلَا يَنظُرُونَ إِلَى ٱلْإِبِلِ كَيْفَ خُلِقَتْ
examples.translation: Do they not look at the camels, how they were created?
examples.note: Two question markers: the أ at the very start ('do they not...?') and كَيْفَ later ('how'). The أ is the easiest one to miss — a single letter doing real grammatical work.

examples.translation: So which of the favors of your Lord will you both deny?
examples.note: أَيّ here means 'which' — picking one item out of a set. This refrain repeats throughout Surah ar-Rahman.

examples.translation: Where did you go? And when will you come back?
examples.note: Two interrogatives in one breath — أَيْنَ for place, مَتَى for time. Both sit at the front of their clauses.
